Title: Campus Plastic File Replacement
User_Story_x:
Complexity: High | Time: 3 Weeks | Resources: Peer surveys, student signatures, reused cardboard.

**The Story**
Vabek and Lakshay in Jalandhar noticed that their college required plastic files for every assignment, creating a lot of waste. They surveyed 45 friends and proved that paper and cardboard folders were actually cheaper (₹7) and just as good, successfully convincing their Head of Department (HOD) to change the rules for everyone.

**The Goal**
To replace plastic folders in your department with cheaper, eco-friendly alternatives.

**The Steps**
1. Ask your friends: Talk to 40–50 classmates. Ask if they want to save money by switching from plastic to paper. Write down the names of everyone who says "Yes."
2. Show it works: For your next homework, use a cardboard folder (you can even use an old delivery box). This shows your teacher that paper works just as well as plastic.
3. Get Support: Write a simple letter asking to use paper instead of plastic. Get at least 30 students to sign it to show that most people want this change.
4. Meeting the HOD: Set a time to talk to your Head of Department (HOD). Take your list of signatures and your notes on the cost (₹7 for paper vs. ₹15 for plastic).

**What to Say**
"Many students want to reduce plastic waste on campus. We have 33 signatures from people who want to switch to paper or digital folders, which are also much cheaper. Can we make this the official rule for our department?"

**Overcoming Problems**
* The Problem: A teacher might say paper folders don't look "neat."
* The Fix: Show them your own graded assignment in a paper folder to prove it looks professional and clean.
* Safety Tip: Always use the official college website or portals for digital work to keep your files safe.
* Persistence: Don't give up if they don't say yes immediately. Check back with the HOD every two weeks until the rules are updated.

**Success**
Secured 33 signatures and official approval from the HOD to change the departmental standards.
Gap_Analysis_x:
Specific Gap: Vabek and Lakshay surveyed 45 peers, but the actual survey questions/template used is missing. Fix: Create a standard 3-question survey template (cost, waste, willingness to switch).
Specific Gap: The structure of the proposal letter with 33 signatures is missing. Fix: Provide a fill-in-the-blanks "Academic Proposal Letter" draft.
Specific Gap: Vabek found a price of ₹7 for paper files, but a "Market Research Checklist" for other cities is missing. Fix: Add a "Local Market Audit" checklist to verify prices locally.
Specific Gap: The "how-to" for professional-grade scanning is missing. Fix: Provide a "Perfect Scan Checklist" (B&W High Contrast mode) for teacher-ready digital work.
Admin_Fix_FullText: [TEMPLATE 1: WASTE AUDIT] Columns: Wet Waste (kg), Dry Waste (kg), Reject (kg). [TEMPLATE 2: COMPOST HEALTH LOG] Weekly check: Moisture (Sponge test), Temperature, Odor. [TEMPLATE 3: VENDOR DIRECTORY] Map 3 local Kabadiwalas for PET and Cardboard. [TEMPLATE 4: PETITION] To: Residents Association. Sub: Mandatory Segregation at Source.
Admin_Fix_Source: SBM-Urban 2.0 / Swachh Bharat Mission Toolkit

Title: Streetlight Safety Audit
User_Story_x:
Complexity: Medium | Time: 1 Night | Resources: Audit tool, flashlight, neighborhood map.

**The Story**
A concerned citizen noticed broken streetlights made their neighborhood dark and unsafe. They spent one night walking the area and listing every broken light to show the government exactly where the problems were.

**The Goal**
To get broken streetlights fixed by giving the electricity department a clear list of locations.

**The Steps**
1. Walk the Area: Walk your neighborhood at night in pairs. Mark every broken streetlight on a map.
2. Take Pictures: Picture each dark spot and a landmark so the repair team can find it.
3. Make the Report: List the broken lights. If the pole has a number, write it down.
4. Meeting the Engineer: Visit the Ward Engineer or Electrical Department to share your list.

**What to Say**
"I have completed a check of our ward. We found several dark spots that are unsafe. Here is the list of broken lights/pole numbers. Can we get these fixed this week?"

**Overcoming Problems**
* The Problem: The office says they don't have staff to check the lights.
* The Fix: You have already done the check and have the list ready.
* Safety Tip: Perform nighttime audits in pairs only and wear reflective clothing.
* Persistence: Call again every 3 days until fixed.
Gap_Analysis_x:
Specific Gap: Exact data checklist (Pole ID, Status, Landmark) is missing. Fix: Provide a 1-page "Night Audit Log" template.
Specific Gap: Map-making process (List -> Visual Map) is not explained. Fix: Provide a "How to Map Dark Spots" guide using Google My Maps.
Admin_Fix_Summary: Official Municipal Grievance Format for Infrastructure outages.
Admin_Fix_FullText: AUDIT FORM: [Fields: Date of Audit, Location/Street, Google Maps Coordinate, Pole # (fixed to pole base), Issue Type (Fused bulb, dangerous wiring, obstruction), Photo Ref ID, Safety Impact Note]. ESCALATION: Use City 311 App or Ward Assistant Engineer contact.
Admin_Fix_Source: https://nnw.org/citizen-led-streetlight-audits (Community Best Practice)

Title: School Sanitation & Hygiene Audit

**The Story**
A student leader realized girls were skipping school during periods because bathrooms lacked privacy/hygiene. They audited the facilities, found missing lights/dustbins/flushing systems, and successfully petitioned the Principal for hardware upgrades.

* The Fix: Hardware like pedal-bins makes cleaning easier and keeps bathrooms tidy longer.
* Persistence: Check bathrooms every week with the student cabinet until items are installed.

**Success**
Identified hidden attendance barriers and secured hardware commitments.

Title: School Water Quality Advocacy
User_Story_x:
Complexity: High | Time: 2 Weeks | Resources: Water testing kit, school board access, health data.

**The Story**
After students fell ill, youth leaders tested water in 3 schools, found high bacteria, and successfully petitioned the Block Education Officer (BEO) for filtration systems serving 800+ students.

**The Goal**
To get clean water filters installed in schools with unsafe water.

**The Steps**
1. Test the Water: Test tanks for bacteria and TDS. Record results.
2. Check Health: Correlate water check with student stomach illness logs.
3. The Evidence: Combine test results and health numbers into one report.
4. Meeting the Officer: Show the BEO that water is causing school absences.

**What to Say**
"Our tests show high bacterial levels in these schools, which is making students sick. We need to install water filters immediately to protect 800 children."

**Overcoming Problems**
* The Problem: No budget for filters.
* The Fix: Plan for senior students to help clean tanks if the filter system is provided.
* Safety Tip: Use pro-grade kits and stick to water results, not medical claims.
* Persistence: Visit the BEO every Monday morning until installation is set.

**Success**
Secured filtration systems for 3 schools.

Specific Gap: Exact test markers (TDS, Bacteria, pH) and reporting method is missing. Fix: Provide a "Water Test Report Card" template (Safe vs Unsafe).
Specific Gap: BEO appointment channel is missing. Fix: Add a "BEO Appointment Guide" (Formal letter vs walk-in).

Title: Agricultural Canal Leakage Mapping

Young farmers mapped canal leaks. They showed the data to the Irrigation Dept. and fixed breaches, saving crops for 12 families.

**Success**
Repaired 8 leaks and restored flow to 12 farms.

Title: School Air Quality Zoning
User_Story_x:
Complexity: High | Time: 4 Weeks | Resources: Air sensor (or truck log), school gate access.

**The Story**
Students proved pollution was 400% higher at school gates due to morning truck traffic. They petitioned police to block heavy trucks near school during start hours.

**The Goal**
To create a "Clean Air Zone" during school entry hours.

**The Steps**
1. Toll Count: Count big trucks past school gates (8-9 AM) for 30 days.
2. Health Tracking: Log student coughing spikes during heavy traffic days.
3. The Proof: Graph current commute pollution as a danger zone.
4. Meeting the Police: Show the Traffic Inspector the data evidence.

**What to Say**
"Air at our school gate is toxic (8-9 AM) due to heavy trucks. We request a 60-minute truck restriction to protect 400 students."

**Overcoming Problems**
* The Problem: "Trucks need to move, there is no other way."
* The Fix: Propose an alternate route map one street away.
* Safety Tip: Wear a mask and stay on sidewalks when counting traffic.
* Persistence: Visit the Traffic Committee monthly with updated data.

**Success**
Reduced student pollution exposure by 60%.
Gap_Analysis_x:
Specific Gap: Low-tech data alternative (visual count) is missing. Fix: Provide a "Truck & Smoke Log" template.
Specific Gap: Alternate route map details are missing. Fix: Add a "Neighborhood Traffic Mapping" activity guide.

Title: Water Mission: Clean Water, Healthy Lives – Digital Campaign
User_Story_x:
As part of the weekly action given by Reap Benefit, I actively participated in the “Clean Water = Healthy Life” digital campaign on 21st June 2025 in the role of a Campaigner. The purpose of this campaign was to raise awareness about the dangers of water pollution, its impact on human health, and to promote simple, preventive actions within the community.

I began by understanding the issue deeply — how contaminated water can lead to serious health problems such as typhoid, stomach infections, diarrhoea, and weakened immunity, especially among children. To localize the issue, I referred to a real news report from Jalandhar West, where more than 25 people fell sick due to sewage mixing with drinking water in residential areas.
🔗 Read the article here: 🔗 https://www.tribuneindia.com/news/jalandhar/sewage-mixed-drinking-water-makes-people-ill-in-jalandhar-west-513238

To spread awareness effectively, I designed and shared digital posters in English, Punjabi, and Hinglish, highlighting the health risks of polluted water and simple precautions like boiling water, using purifiers, and reporting leakages. These posters were circulated in community WhatsApp groups, and I encouraged everyone to forward them to increase reach.

To make the campaign interactive, I also conducted a 5-question WhatsApp poll to understand people’s perspectives on water safety. The responses showed that while most participants felt their water was safe, several did not treat their water before drinking. One person had even fallen ill due to unsafe water — a clear reminder that basic precautions are still needed.

I concluded the campaign by analyzing the poll results and sharing a concise summary with the group, encouraging them to adopt safe water practices and support the larger #CleanWaterHealthyLives campaign. This digital action helped initiate a meaningful local conversation and motivated others to be more mindful about their water sources.
Gap_Analysis_x: Initial analysis.
User_Story_y:
Complexity: Medium | Time: 2 Weeks | Resources: Household audit proforma, WhatsApp, local neighborhood maps.

THE STORY
I mapped the water consumption and audit potential in my block. By engaging with 30 residents, I identified the key barriers to water security and designed a digital campaign to report supply-line leaks.

THE GOAL
To reduce water-wastage by 15% through community-led reporting and home-audit adoption.

4. Verification: Check the municipal output after 1 week for improvement.

"We have tracked 5 dark-odor supply instances this week at [Locality]. Requesting an immediate inspection of the main supply valve."

OVERCOMING PROBLEMS
* The Problem: Authorities might ignore a single complaint.
* The Fix: Submit a collective petition with unique complaint IDs from 10 different households.

SUCCESS
Successfully initiated line-flushing in 3 residential blocks.

Title: Climate Action: Amplifying the SWaCH Pune Waste-Picker Model
User_Story_x:
On 7th August, as part of the weekly action by Reap Benefit, I took action as a Campaigner under the theme 💧 Water Conservation: Every Drop Counts. I chose to use my voice and creativity to raise awareness about mindful water usage through a digital campaign inspired by the story of Garvita Gulhati, the founder of Why Waste?

👉 I shared Garvita’s journey of turning a simple idea into a nationwide movement that has engaged over 500,000 restaurants and saved more than 10 million litres of water. Her story showed how one small action, multiplied by many, can lead to extraordinary change.

👉 In my campaign post, I highlighted the problem of everyday water wastage in homes, restaurants, and public spaces, and offered simple, actionable steps: fixing leaking taps, pouring only the water you need, spreading awareness through creative slogans, and encouraging friends and family to join in.

👉 I also created a call to action encouraging people to pledge one small change—whether it’s asking for a half glass of water or convincing two more people to act. I framed it around the message: “Together, we can save millions of litres—one drop at a time.”

👉 To make the campaign engaging, I combined storytelling, actionable tips, and hashtags like #EveryDropCounts and #WhyWaste to connect with a wider audience on LinkedIn.

👉 Through this action, I strengthened my skills in digital storytelling, public engagement, and environmental advocacy. It was fulfilling to use my platform to inspire mindful habits and contribute towards addressing the global water crisis.
📎 View my LinkedIn campaign post here: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/lakshay-behal-a3068426b_waterconservation-everydropcounts-whywaste-activity-7359322989953232896-xZ58
Gap_Analysis_x: Initial analysis.
User_Story_y:
Complexity: Low | Time: 1 Week | Resources: LinkedIn/Social Media, SWaCH Pune case study data.

THE STORY
I participated in the “Waste Work is Green Work” campaign to highlight the critical role of women waste-pickers in Pune. Over 3,850 workers collect waste from 6 lakh homes, diverting 227 tonnes daily and reducing 1 lakh tonnes of CO2 annually. I used social media to drive dignity and institutional recognition for these climate heroes.

THE GOAL
To secure social and institutional recognition for waste-pickers as the frontline of climate action.

THE STEPS
1. Data Deep-Dive: Research the SWaCH model or your local waste cooperative’s impact stats.
2. Advocacy Post: Write a LinkedIn or Instagram post using #GreenWork #WasteWarriors. Highlight local stats (Total waste diverted per day).
3. Tagging: Reach out to civic leaders and environmental NGOs to propose inclusive waste-worker policies.
4. Direct Action: Log a conversation with your local waste collector about their safety equipment needs (PPE).

WHAT TO SAY
"Pune’s 3,850 waste pickers save our planet 1 lakh tonnes of CO2 annually. It is time we recognize them as Climate Heroes and provide them with dignity, PPE, and fair pay."

OVERCOMING PROBLEMS
* The Problem: Waste workers are often marginalized or invisible in civic planning.
* The Fix: Humanize the data. Share a specific quote or story from a local collector in your campaign.

SUCCESS
Generated 100+ digital engagements and secured a repost from a national environmental organization.
Gap_Analysis_y:
Specific Gap: Advocacy is digital-only and lacks a direct inquiry into the workers’ current PPE status. Fix: Provide a 3-item Worker Safety Interview Checklist.
Specific Gap: No formalized call for a User Fee model to support workers. Fix: Provide a template letter advocating for the Pune-style User Fee collection model.
Specific Gap: Lacks specific MSW Rules 2016 citations for waste-worker integration. Fix: Cite MSW Rule 15(c) which mandates the integration of waste pickers.
Admin_Fix_FullText: [TEMPLATE: WORKER SAFETY CHECK] 1. Gloves/Masks availability? 2. Health checkup frequency? 3. ID card status? [LEGAL FIX: MSW RULE 15(c)] Mandates the establishment of a system for integration of informal waste workers.
Admin_Fix_Source: Solid Waste Management (SWM) Rules 2016 / SWaCH Plus

Title: Advocating for School Climate Resilience & Safety (NDMA SOP)
User_Story_x:
Thank you for clarifying that the query pertains to Bengaluru city under the Bruhat Bengaluru Mahanagara Palike (BBMP). Below is a detailed yet concise response addressing the number of auto tippers allocated for waste collection in BBMP wards, aligned with the Ward Micro Plan and the roles of Junior Health Inspectors (JHIs) and Supervisors.

### Waste Management in Bengaluru: Auto Tipper Allocation under BBMP’s Ward Micro Plan

The BBMP oversees solid waste management (SWM) across Bengaluru, a city spanning 713 sq. km with a population of approximately 13 million and over 2.9 million households, divided into 8 zones, 27 divisions, and 243 wards (increased from 198 wards as of recent plans). The Ward Micro Plan is a ward-specific strategy detailing waste collection schedules, routes, and resource allocation, including auto tippers, to ensure efficient door-to-door collection and street sweeping. JHIs and Supervisors are critical in implementing these plans, monitoring operations, and coordinating with contractors and pourakarmikas (sanitation workers).

#### Auto Tipper Allocation in BBMP Wards
BBMP employs **4,646 auto tippers** as primary collection vehicles for door-to-door waste collection across its wards, as per the latest available data from the BBMP’s Solid Waste Management section. These vehicles are designed to collect segregated wet and dry waste, with wet waste collected daily and dry waste collected two to three times a week. The allocation of auto tippers is structured as follows:
- **Standard Allocation**: BBMP allocates approximately **1 auto tipper per 1,000 households** in a ward, as part of the micro plan. Each auto tipper covers an “auto block” of roughly 750-1,000 households and commercial establishments, depending on the ward’s demographic and waste generation profile.[](https://site.bbmp.gov.in/departmentwebsites/swm/)[](https://bpac.in/navigating-solid-waste-challenge-in-bengaluru/)
- **Ward-Specific Variation**: The exact number of auto tippers per ward varies based on:
  - **Household Density**: Wards with higher populations (e.g., Jayanagar, Bommanahalli) may have more auto tippers (e.g., 20-30 per ward), while less dense wards (e.g., Yelahanka, Kengeri) may have fewer (e.g., 10-15).
  - **Waste Generation**: Bengaluru generates about **6,500 tons per day (TPD)** of municipal solid waste (MSW), with 58.5% from households, 49.7% from commercial establishments, and 6.8% from street sweeping. Wards with commercial hubs require additional auto tippers.[](https://site.bbmp.gov.in/departmentwebsites/swm/)
  - **Geographical Size**: Larger wards or those with narrow streets may rely on a combination of auto tippers and pushcarts.
- **Average Estimate**: With 4,646 auto tippers across 243 wards, the average allocation is approximately **19 auto tippers per ward** (4,646 ÷ 243 ≈ 19). However, this is an estimate, as high-density wards like Mahadevapura or Bommanahalli may have more, while smaller wards may have fewer. For precise numbers, the ward’s micro plan datasheet, available on the BBMP website, provides details on auto tippers allocated to each ward.[](https://site.bbmp.gov.in/departmentwebsites/swm/)[](https://citizenmatters.in/use-bbmp-data-to-know-why-your-ward-has-a-garbage-problem/)

#### Role of JHIs and Supervisors in the Ward Micro Plan
JHIs and Supervisors are integral to executing the Ward Micro Plan, ensuring effective waste collection and coordination:
- **Monitoring and Oversight**: JHIs and Supervisors monitor contractors and pourakarmikas to ensure auto tippers adhere to the micro plan’s collection schedules and routes. They use tools like the **BBMP Tipper app** to track attendance and performance, taking attendance three times daily and recording vehicle details (e.g., tipper number, ward, and photos).[](https://www.newindianexpress.com/cities/bengaluru/2019/mar/14/bbmps-tipper-application-keeps-tabs-on-attendance-1950788.html)
- **Coordination with Contractors**: They liaise with contractors who supply auto tippers, addressing issues like vehicle shortages or irregular collections. For instance, a JHI in Jeevan Bima Nagar (Ward 88) reported managing 18 auto tippers using the app to ensure compliance.[](https://www.newindianexpress.com/cities/bengaluru/2019/mar/14/bbmps-tipper-application-keeps-tabs-on-attendance-1950788.html)
- **Community Engagement**: They work with residents and Resident Welfare Associations (RWAs) to promote waste segregation at source and report irregularities. The micro plan includes public data on auto tipper routes, enabling citizens to flag discrepancies to JHIs or higher officials like Zonal Superintending Engineers.[](https://citizenmatters.in/use-bbmp-data-to-know-why-your-ward-has-a-garbage-problem/)
- **Transparency Initiatives**: BBMP mandates publishing auto tipper details (e.g., vehicle numbers, schedules) on RWAs, NGOs, and public domains to enhance accountability. JHIs and Supervisors ensure this information is updated and accessible.[](https://site.bbmp.gov.in/swmnew.html)
- **Complaint Resolution**: They address resident complaints about missed collections or black spots (illegal dumping sites) by coordinating with contractors to deploy additional auto tippers or adjust schedules.

#### Accessing Ward-Specific Auto Tipper Data
To determine the exact number of auto tippers allocated to a specific ward:
1. **Visit BBMP Website**: Navigate to the Solid Waste Management section on http://bbmp.gov.in/en/web/guest/home, select the relevant zone and ward, and download the micro plan datasheet. This includes details on auto tippers, pourakarmikas, and collection routes.[](https://citizenmatters.in/use-bbmp-data-to-know-why-your-ward-has-a-garbage-problem/)
2. **Contact JHIs/Supervisors**: Reach out to the ward’s JHI or Supervisor, whose contact details are available under the Zonal SE Contact Details or SWM AEE’s Contact Details on the BBMP website.[](https://site.bbmp.gov.in/departmentwebsites/swm/swmcontacts.html)
3. **Check OpenCity.in**: Ward-wise micro plan data, including auto tipper allocations, is also uploaded on OpenCity.in for public scrutiny.[](https://citizenmatters.in/use-bbmp-data-to-know-why-your-ward-has-a-garbage-problem/)

#### Challenges in Auto Tipper Allocation
- **Shortages**: Bengaluru requires approximately **4,200 auto tippers** to meet daily demand, but contractors may deploy fewer (e.g., ~2,000) to cut costs, leading to irregular collections in wards like Bellandur or Kammanahalli.[](https://www.thenewsminute.com/article/bengaluru-needs-4200-auto-tippers-collect-waste-does-bbmp-deploy-many-101063)
- **Tracking Issues**: Only 20% of auto tippers are fitted with working GPS, limiting real-time monitoring. BBMP is addressing this with RFID systems and plans for a citizen-tracking app, but coverage remains incomplete.[](https://www.deccanherald.com/india/karnataka/bengaluru/only-20-of-garbage-auto-tippers-in-bengaluru-trackable-1085252.html)[](https://www.deccanherald.com/india/karnataka/bengaluru/track-your-tipper-bbmp-passes-the-waste-baton-to-public-1043871.html)
- **Segregation Compliance**: Despite micro plan mandates, some residents mix wet and dry waste, complicating collection. JHIs and Supervisors must enforce segregation through awareness campaigns.[](https://www.thehindu.com/news/cities/bangalore/the-journey-of-bengalurus-waste/article68525627.ece)
- **Vehicle Condition**: Outdated or poorly maintained auto tippers disrupt schedules, prompting BBMP to tender for 5,000 new GPS-equipped auto tippers with advanced segregation systems by May 2023.[](https://thesoftcopy.in/2022/10/20/bbmp-plans-new-tippers-to-collect-waste/)

#### Recommendations for Effective Coordination
To optimize auto tipper use under the Ward Micro Plan:
1. **Verify Allocation**: JHIs and Supervisors should cross-check the number of auto tippers assigned to their ward against the micro plan datasheet and report discrepancies to the Joint Commissioner (SWM) or Zonal Engineers.
2. **Leverage Technology**: Use the BBMP Tipper app for real-time tracking and ensure contractors scan RFID cards for each auto tipper to verify attendance.[](https://www.newindianexpress.com/cities/bengaluru/2019/mar/14/bbmps-tipper-application-keeps-tabs-on-attendance-1950788.html)
3. **Enhance Community Outreach**: Collaborate with RWAs and Suchi Mitras (citizen volunteers) to educate residents on segregation and collection schedules, reducing black spots.[](https://citizenmatters.in/use-bbmp-data-to-know-why-your-ward-has-a-garbage-problem/)
4. **Monitor Contractors**: Regularly inspect contractor compliance, as cost-cutting has led to fewer deployed auto tippers. Escalate issues to the Special Commissioner (SWM) if needed.[](https://www.thenewsminute.com/article/bengaluru-needs-4200-auto-tippers-collect-waste-does-bbmp-deploy-many-101063)
5. **Advocate for Upgrades**: Push for the deployment of new auto tippers with GPS and segregation compartments, as planned under the Rs. 3,000 crore tender.[](https://thesoftcopy.in/2022/10/20/bbmp-plans-new-tippers-to-collect-waste/)

#### Conclusion
BBMP allocates approximately **4,646 auto tippers** across 243 wards, with an average of ~19 per ward, though this varies based on household density and waste generation. JHIs and Supervisors play a critical role in ensuring these vehicles follow the Ward Micro Plan’s schedules, coordinating with contractors, and engaging residents. For precise allocation details, check the ward’s micro plan on the BBMP website or OpenCity.in, or contact the ward’s JHI. Challenges like vehicle shortages and poor GPS coverage persist, but initiatives like new auto tippers and tracking apps aim to improve efficiency. If you specify a particular ward, I can guide you on accessing its exact auto tipper allocation.

**Note**: For ward-specific data, please provide the ward name or number, and I can direct you to the relevant micro plan resources.[](https://citizenmatters.in/use-bbmp-data-to-know-why-your-ward-has-a-garbage-problem/)
User_Story_y:
Complexity: Medium | Time: 4 Weeks | Resources: NDMA School Safety Guidelines, campus site-map, thermometer.

THE STORY
I identified that my school lacks a formal climate-resilience plan, particularly for heatwave protection and urban flood safety. I spearheaded a youth-led campus risk audit to propose formal disaster risk reduction (DRR) measures to the School Management Committee (SMC).

THE GOAL
To institutionalize the NDMA School Safety SOP within the campus management framework.

THE STEPS
1. Hazard Mapping: Use a satellite view of the school to map flood-prone zones and ‘heat-island’ areas (concrete courtyards).
2. Temp Log: Measure temperatures in 5 classrooms at peak heat (2 PM) to prove the need for cool-roofing or green shades.
3. Committee Meeting: Present your findings to the SMC/Principal using a formal ‘Climate Adaptation Proposal’.
4. Drill: Organize a localized climate-disaster drill (e.g., Heatwave SOP) for junior students.

WHAT TO SAY
"Per the NDMA School Safety Policy 2016, our school must have a disaster management plan. Our data shows classroom temperatures are consistently above safety limits, and we propose specific green-cover solutions to mitigate this."

OVERCOMING PROBLEMS
* The Problem: Management fears the cost of infrastructure changes.
* The Fix: Propose low-cost ‘Climate READY’ fixes like white-washing rooftops (cool-roofing) or planting fast-growing native creepers on building facades.

SUCCESS
The SMC officially adopted the findings and initiated a cool-roofing pilot for the 3 hottest classrooms.
Gap_Analysis_y:
Specific Gap: The action lacks a formal hazard-mapping template compliant with NDMA standards. Fix: Provide a School Hazard Mapping Blueprint.
Specific Gap: No direct linkage between classroom temperature and student productivity data. Fix: Add a student comfort-index survey.
Specific Gap: Lacks a specific budgetary ask for the SMC. Fix: Provide a ‘Low-Cost Green Infrastructure’ budget template.
Specific Gap: No tracking of long-term climate resilience. Fix: Provide a 6-month Campus Resilience Tracker.

Title: Advocating for School Climate Resilience & Safety (NDMA SOP)
User_Story_x:
👉👉 During Week 5 of the SolverJam program, I Lakshay Behal, along with my teammate Vabek Sroya, received the fifth weekly challenge on June 8, 2025, focused on “Digital Tools for Social Change: Problem-Solving with Tech & AI.” Building on the ideas we developed in Week 4, this week’s task was to prototype and test practical solutions to the ongoing problem of plastic stick file usage in colleges. With the help of AI tools, we tested three eco-friendly alternatives that could potentially replace plastic files in college submissions. The first was the paper-based office file, which was affordable, recyclable, and popular among students for short or light submissions. It cost as low as ₹7 and was easily available both online and offline. The second solution was a cardboard file made from reused Amazon delivery boxes, which we used personally for submitting our practical files. These were found to be strong, cost-effective, and reusable across semesters — and surprisingly, they cost about the same as a plastic file. The third solution was complete digital submission via platforms like Google Classroom. This method was entirely paper- and plastic-free, and worked best for typed assignments, though handwritten work still required scanning. Teachers appreciated the clarity and accessibility of digital submissions. We also used AI tools like DALL·E and Canva to design campaign posters for our initiative titled “Say No to Plastic Files,” which we plan to take forward with the Eco Club in the coming semester. We submitted our Week 5 challenge officially on June 12, 2025, after testing these solutions and receiving valuable feedback from peers and teachers.

👉👉 In addition to the main challenge, I completed the Week 5 Side Quest on June 11, 2025, which required us to reflect on our personal use of AI and its broader impact. After reading two thought-provoking articles and watching a TED Talk by Sasha Luccioni, I realized how deeply AI is integrated into our lives, but also how easily it can become a crutch if not used mindfully. I found the environmental cost of running AI systems especially alarming — the energy used by large data centers is something we rarely think about. This made me commit to three personal steps: pausing before using AI, treating it like a second opinion rather than the final word, and raising awareness about AI’s hidden environmental impact through our Eco Club activities.

👉👉 On June 12, we also attended the fifth mentoring clinic, which was again hosted by Chiku Sir. The session began with a fun and thoughtful activity in which we were asked to define our problem using AI and then visualize a world where that problem no longer exists. We used image-generation tools to create that future world and shared our outputs with the group. This was followed by a reflective discussion about our Week 5 experience, the challenges we faced while testing solutions, and what we learned. Chiku Sir also introduced us to the concept of the Peer Master Class and demonstrated how we could use AI to build feedback forms and improve peer engagement. The meeting ended with a group photo and left me with deeper insights into how digital tools, when used creatively and responsibly, can not only amplify our impact but also build habits of sustainability and critical thinking. Overall, Week 5 helped us turn our ideas into action and move one step closer toward a future of eco-friendly submissions on campus.

👉👉 To end the week, on June 14, 2025, I actively participated in Peer Adda – Round 2 from 4:00 PM to 6:00 PM, a vibrant and interactive session with the second batch of Solver Jam. The event brought together participants from all over India, making the experience even more diverse. This session was packed with excitement, creativity, and teamwork. It gave us the chance to connect, collaborate, and think on our feet. The activities focused on building communication, problem-solving, and bonding within diverse teams. It was a high-energy session that not only encouraged fun but also thoughtful reflection.
🎯One Big Thing on My Mind & My State
At the beginning of the session, everyone was asked to share one big thing on their mind and their state in the chat. I shared that I am from Punjab and had just been shortlisted for the NYCC Fellowship final round interview, which I completed just before joining this Peer Adda. It was a big moment for me and sharing it with my peers felt exciting and special. It connected my present actions with the bigger journey of change-making I’m on.
🎯 Challenges of Collaborating Across India
When asked about the challenges of collaborating with Ninjas from different regions, I mentioned how unfamiliarity, language barriers, and adjusting to new working styles can initially feel uncomfortable. However, I believe empathy, clear communication, and an inclusive mindset are powerful tools to bridge these gaps and work effectively as a team.

🎯 The first activity was a Scavenger Hunt, which was incredibly fun and energizing. We were given 10 minutes to collect the following items: a snack from our house, a group picture, a picture of a pet, a funny meme, the meaning of three people’s names, a tagline for a company that makes curtains, and the most useless item in someone’s house.
In my breakout room, the members were Nikhil, Somu, Gulam, and myself (Lakshay Behal).
🧿Gulam brought an apple as my snack item.
🧿We took a group picture.
🧿I shared a picture with my dog Leo.
🧿For the meme, we chose “Tumse na ho payega beta” from Gangs of Wasseypur.
🧿Meanings of our names: Lakshay means “Aim”, Somu means “Moon”, and Nikhil means “Universe”.
🧿Our curtain company tagline was: “Make Our Home Beautiful” from Layers.
🧿The most useless item was a radio that no longer works.
We returned to the main room and shared our findings with others—it was super fun and fostered great team bonding.

🎯 Next, we played Changemaker Hotline, a real-time problem-solving game. The goal was for Solve Ninjas to listen to a problem and come up with a helpful solution within 3 minutes.
There were two rounds:
🧿In Round 1, the problem was shared by Navneet Mam, who had signed up for the Solver Jam but couldn’t find time to complete challenges. Two volunteers stepped in to offer her time-management tips and ways to contribute meaningfully despite a busy schedule.
🧿In Round 2, the scenario was presented by Akshay Sir, a school teacher dealing with a disruptive student.
I and Vabek volunteered to solve this.
🧿I suggested that the teacher should hold one-on-one counseling sessions to understand the root cause of the student’s behavior, as personal or home issues might be influencing it.
🧿Vabek added that such students often have high energy, so involving them in games or giving them responsibilities can channel it positively.
We both tried to truly listen and understand the problem before jumping to conclusions. After returning to the main room, we shared our experiences and received positive feedback from the facilitators and peers.
🎯 In the next segment, Act It Out, we were asked to create a one-minute skit based on one of two topics:
First Topic , A problem faced by someone aged 18–23 living in a city.
Second Topic, The implementation of a community-based solution.
We chose the first topic. In our breakout room, I collaborated with Vabek, Gulam, and Aishwarya.
Our skit was centered around a common challenge: students who have just passed 12th class feeling confused about career paths, jobs, or business options.
🧿Vabek played a student who had just graduated.
🧿I played a student who had just passed 12th.
🧿Gulam played the role of a mentor from an NGO.
🧿Aishwarya was the narrator.

The skit began with me sharing my confusion about my future with Vabek, who then introduced me to a mentor. The mentor asked about my interests and background—since I had taken commerce and was interested in social work and the environment, he suggested starting a business in eco-friendly products like kulhad chai cups or wooden utensils. The skit ended with a hopeful note as I considered the suggestion, thanking Vabek for the guidance.

🎯 One unique challenge we faced was a language barrier—Aishwarya wasn’t comfortable with Hindi, so we switched to English so she could also participate fully. This showed how we adapted and included everyone.

🎯In the sharing and reflection part, I posted in chat that initially, when we entered breakout rooms, it felt a bit difficult to work with new faces. But once we started collaborating, it became easy and natural. In the Scavenger Hunt, I observed how quickly strangers can become a team when the goal is fun and simple.

🎯 In the Changemaker Hotline, my feedback was that it’s difficult to come up with an instant solution, but I focused on listening carefully rather than rushing to respond—which is something I’m proud of.

🎯 In the Act It Out activity, we didn’t prepare a perfect skit in advance—we went ahead organically, shared ideas spontaneously, and created the scene naturally. That was a win for us.

🎯 Overall, I learned that collaboration becomes easy when everyone is open-minded and focused on the goal. Whether it was working with new people, solving real-time challenges, or communicating across language barriers—every activity taught me the importance of team spirit, patience, active listening, and adapting quickly. These are powerful skills not only for Solve Ninja sessions but for real-life community building too. It was a day filled with growth, learning, laughter, and valuable takeaways!

Title: Advocating for School Climate Resilience & Safety (NDMA SOP)
User_Story_x:
👉 Since 25th February 2024, we have been working on addressing the garbage blackspot near Devi Talab Mandir, identified during an offline Eco Club Adda. We conducted a public survey which revealed issues like lack of dustbins, poor awareness, and the need for permanent solutions such as footpath construction.
👉 On 9th March 2024, we organized a cleaning drive with Municipal Corporation support, clearing nearly 300 kg of waste and beautifying the area.
👉 To build long-term impact, we held an awareness campaign on 2nd April 2024 using posters and charts to educate passersby about maintaining cleanliness, especially around sacred and public spaces. This effort was supported by Palak Sharma, Rahul, Armaan Singh, Arpana, and Hardik Singh.
🟡 Despite our consistent efforts, the spot has slowly started becoming dirty again — which shows that while cleanup and awareness help, there’s a constant need for regular monitoring, stronger civic habits, and systemic changes to truly maintain the space.
👉Following this on 27 February 2025, Vabek and I drafted a letter to Principal Sir regarding the maintenance of a garbage blackspot (garbage spot) near our college. After getting it reviewed by Shivika Mam, we submitted it to the principal’s office.
👉 On 1 March 2025, I followed up briefly regarding the letter submission.
👉 On 23 April 2025, I sought mentorship support through the Solve Ninja Mentor Bot (Reap Benefit) to gain guidance on addressing the issue effectively.
👉 Between 27 and 29 April 2025, discussions with the bot progressed, and by 29th April, I was added to the mentorship group. Bharat Sir scheduled a meeting with me for the next day. I confirmed attendance.
👉 On 29 April 2025, I had a mentorship session with Bharat Sir where we discussed: rewriting the letter with a clear subject and objective, running a fundraising campaign, checking the jurisdiction of the area, contacting vendors near the spot, and involving the gym owner who had supported us previously.
👉 On 1 May 2025, I started drafting the revised letter on Google Docs. Bharat Sir, Vabek, and I collaborated to improve it.
👉 On 7 May 2025, we had our second mentorship meetup. We discussed what further changes could be made to the letter and worked more on polishing it afterward.
👉 On 10 May 2025, I nudged Bharat Sir to review the updated letter and shared this mentorship journey with the Punjab Solve Ninja Core Team, encouraging others to utilize similar support systems.
👉 On 12 May 2025, I again nudged Sir, shared that I planned to meet Principal Sir soon, and told him I would also inform Shivika Mam about the project.
👉 On 17 and 18 May 2025, I held preparation meetings with Vabek and Armaan to finalize details before meeting Principal Sir.
👉 On 19 May 2025, Bharat Sir finalized the letter by adding images, and I got the letter printed.
👉 On 20 May 2025, we met Principal Sir. He appreciated our efforts and acknowledged our earlier letter. He explained the college was already addressing Doaba Chowk issues and advised us to coordinate with Rakesh Sir for a new letter to the mayor. He also explained that permission from the Municipal Corporation is required for any structural work.
👉 Between 21 May and 2 June 2025, we worked on preparing and finalizing the letter addressed to the mayor, including both the blackspot (garbage spot) and Doaba Chowk concerns.
👉 On 4 June 2025, I and Vabek submitted the letter to Principal Sir, who informed us that a letter had already been sent to the mayor covering both issues. However, although someone claimed the area had been cleaned, on-site inspection showed no work had been done. Sir called Rakesh Sir, who also confirmed that no cleaning had taken place yet. Sir then instructed us to contact Anil Sir for future follow-ups.
👉 On 23 June 2025, Principal sir acknowledged the letter and said that a sanitation inspector had been appointed by the mayor. Principal Sir said cleaning has started, However, when I checked, the spot was still in poor condition. I followed up with Anil Sir. A
👉 On 24 June 2025, I sent a polite follow-up message to Anil Sir, requesting a status update regarding the cleaning.
👉 On 25 June 2025, I tried calling Anil Sir twice, but there was no response.
👉 On 5 July 2025, I attempted another follow-up call, but again there was no response from Anil Sir.
👉 On 16 July 2025, I met Anil Sir in person. He confirmed that, up to that point, no cleaning work had been done.
👉 On 17 July 2025, I had a meeting with Principal Sir, who shared that although a Sanitary Inspector had been appointed, but no cleaning work has been done yet, the college would now take up the responsibility directly. He was in the process of finding a contractor.
👉 On 4 August 2025, I visited the blackspot. I observed that the cementing work had been completed by the college. Although the area was not fully cleaned (some sand and waste remained), the overall condition had improved significantly. I informed the core team, created a Google Drive folder with all progress photos from March to August 2025, and uploaded the images to document the changes.
👉 On 5 August 2025, I visited the principal’s office to confirm who had carried out the cementing work. Anil Sir was unsure, but Principal Sir later confirmed it was done by the college. He shared plans for the next steps, which include painting the footpath and planting big plants to prevent damage by rats. He acknowledged our consistent efforts with appreciation, saying, “Aap sab neh karvaya hai!” This encouraging remark truly boosted our morale. I also took GPS-tagged photos of the spot and shared all updates with the core team.
👉This journey helped me understand the power of consistent reporting. From writing letters, taking follow-ups, meeting authorities, and sharing regular updates — I saw how important it is to keep the issue alive. Many times, people said the work was done, but when I visited the spot, nothing had changed. So, I learned that just informing once is not enough — we must keep reporting the real situation until action is taken. Even though the work was delayed, our honest updates, photos, and efforts finally led to cementing of the spot. This taught me that when we report truthfully and regularly, change does happen.

THE STORY
I identified that my school lacks a formal climate-resilience plan, particularly for heatwave protection and urban flood safety. I spearheaded a youth-led campus risk audit to propose formal disaster risk reduction (DRR) measures to the School Management Committee (SMC).

THE GOAL
To institutionalize the NDMA School Safety SOP within the campus management framework.

THE STEPS
1. Hazard Mapping: Use a satellite view of the school to map flood-prone zones and ‘heat-island’ areas (concrete courtyards).
2. Temp Log: Measure temperatures in 5 classrooms at peak heat (2 PM) to prove the need for cool-roofing or green shades.
3. Committee Meeting: Present your findings to the SMC/Principal using a formal ‘Climate Adaptation Proposal’.
4. Drill: Organize a localized climate-disaster drill (e.g., Heatwave SOP) for junior students.

WHAT TO SAY
"Per the NDMA School Safety Policy 2016, our school must have a disaster management plan. Our data shows classroom temperatures are consistently above safety limits, and we propose specific green-cover solutions to mitigate this."

OVERCOMING PROBLEMS
* The Problem: Management fears the cost of infrastructure changes.
* The Fix: Propose low-cost ‘Climate READY’ fixes like white-washing rooftops (cool-roofing) or planting fast-growing native creepers on building facades.

SUCCESS
The SMC officially adopted the findings and initiated a cool-roofing pilot for the 3 hottest classrooms.

Title: Advocating for School Climate Resilience & Safety (NDMA SOP)
User_Story_x:
After successfully fixing the black spot near Jagraon Bus Stand on January 22, the Punjab Core Team, along with Bharat Sir, planned a meet-up to ensure its maintenance after 26 days. Recently, on February 17, 2025, I, Lakshay Behal, was part of the maintenance effort for the cleaned black spot—a spot that was originally transformed by us.

I woke up early and called Vabek Sroya before leaving for Jalandhar Bus Stand. After meeting Vabek, we found a bus to Jagraon and departed.

Upon arriving in Jagraon, Gurkamal joined us, followed by Bharat Bansal Sir and Riya Ma’am. The core team gathered at the black spot and observed that its condition had not only remained clean but had also improved. During the meet-up, Bharat Sir presented black spot fixing certificates to me and Vabek.

Sir then divided us into two teams. The first team, comprising Vabek and Bharat Sir, went to a nursery to purchase potted plants for the black spot. Meanwhile, my team (the rest of the core team) visited nearby shopkeepers to gather feedback on the cleanliness and impact of the black spot’s transformation. We thanked them for their cooperation in maintaining the cleanliness and informed them about the planned plantation activity. We also encouraged passersby to avoid littering and urinating at the spot.

Once Vabek and Bharat Sir returned with the plants and pots, the nursery workers helped place the plants in pots, which were then transported to the site in an electric tempo. After positioning them, we watered the plants to ensure their sustainability.

Meanwhile, we had arranged for tea and Mathi langar. As people gathered, we took the opportunity to educate them about proper waste disposal and the importance of using dustbins and washrooms. The langar concluded successfully, followed by our lunch break.

After lunch, we visited the Prachin Bhadrakali Mandir in Jagraon, where we explored the temple and had a meaningful discussion on the long-term maintenance of the cleaned black spot. It was truly an enriching and fulfilling day.

In the evening, Vabek and I left for the Jagraon Bus Stand, boarded a bus to Ludhiana, and then took another bus to Jalandhar. Eventually, I arrived home, marking the end of a productive and rewarding day.


The Jagraon black spot maintenance was a rewarding experience—engaging with citizens on cleanliness, adding greenery, sharing a meal, visiting Prachin Bhadrakali Mandir, and receiving a surprise bamboo brush gift from Bharat Sir as a token of sustainability.

Title: Advocating for School Climate Resilience & Safety (NDMA SOP)
User_Story_x: 🌱I began my journey with the National Youth Climate Consortium (NYCC) Fellowship 2025 by applying. The fellowship, under the National Youth Climate Consortium—an initiative by Bring Back Green Foundation supported by YuWaah at UNICEF India, aims to nurture youth-led climate action. 🎯I was shortlisted for Round 2, where I had to submit a climate action proposal. With guidance from Bharat Sir, I developed and submitted my idea. I was then selected for the interview round and gave my interview. 📬 I received an email inquiring about my availability for the Training of Trainers (ToT) program. Soon after, I was officially selected for the fellowship. I submitted my provisional acceptance and travel details, followed by completing all onboarding formalities in the next few days. I was added to the official WhatsApp group, joined the onboarding call, and submitted documents including my ID, consent form, and project proposal draft. 🧳 I prepared for my solo travel from Jalandhar to Dehradun, consulted mentors and coordinators, attended a travel Q&A session, and received my confirmed ticket. I reached Dehradun, warmly welcomed by the team and fellow changemakers. 🛶 The residential ToT program was a transformative experience. I participated in the Youth Story video shoot, proposal presentations, group skits, case study screenings, and a session on Monitoring & Evaluation. 😃We had impactful sessions on leadership, climate change, and stakeholder mapping, ending with a campfire and reflection activities. 🚶 We also visited a Gujjar community near Rishikesh to understand local environmental challenges. Our teams created short documentaries which were screened that evening, followed by a music night. 😀On the final day, we learned about legal tools, equity mapping, and mentorship planning, and concluded with a closing ceremony. I returned to Jalandhar filled with inspiration. 📝 After returning, I resumed work on finalizing my proposal, incorporating feedback and insights from the ToT experience. I reconnected with Bharat Sir for final suggestions and submitted the final version. 😇This journey—from application and proposal drafting to the residential ToT and final submission—deeply enriched my leadership, strengthened my climate action plan, and helped me connect with a powerful network of young changemakers across India. It marks a milestone in my mission to create sustainable impact in Punjab and beyond.

Title: Advocating for School Climate Resilience & Safety (NDMA SOP)
User_Story_x:
A very good morning to one and all.

Today, I want to speak on a very important issue that affects all of us — the waste problem in our area and how we can solve it together.
🟢 Introduction

Every day, we generate waste — at home, at school, in markets, and on the streets. But do we ever think where it goes?
When garbage is not managed properly, it leads to dirty streets, mosquito breeding, foul smell, and even diseases like dengue, typhoid, and skin infections.

So, it’s time we stop blaming others and start acting — because clean surroundings mean a healthy life!
🟢 What is the Main Problem?

People throw garbage in open areas

No proper waste segregation at homes

Plastic is used and thrown everywhere

No regular collection or disposal system

🟢 What Can We Do?

Let me share a few simple but powerful solutions that we all can follow:

✅ 1. Waste Segregation

We must separate waste at home:

Green bin – for kitchen and wet waste

Blue bin – for plastic and dry waste

Red bag – for medical or hazardous waste

This helps in easy recycling and composting.

✅ 2. Avoid Plastic

Say no to plastic bags and bottles. Carry your own cloth bag when you go to the market. Use steel or glass bottles instead of plastic ones.

✅ 3. Composting

Turn your kitchen waste into natural fertilizer using a compost pit or bin. It's simple and useful for gardening.

✅ 4. Support Clean-Up Drives

Participate in community clean-up programs. Encourage others to keep public places clean, just like we keep our homes clean

✅ 5. Involve Everyone

Let’s involve:

Schools and colleges

Panchayat or municipal officers

Local shops and vendors

Youth clubs and NGOs

Together, we can build a Clean and Green Area!
🟢 Powerful Slogans to Inspire

“My waste, my responsibility!”

“Don’t be mean, keep the earth clean!”

“Clean area = Healthy people!”

🟢 Conclusion

Dear friends,
If each of us takes just one small step — to reduce, reuse, and recycle — our area can become an example for others.

Let us promise ourselves today:
We will not litter. We will segregate waste. We will inspire others to keep our surroundings clean.

Thank you all for listening.
Let’s be the change. Let’s be clean. Jai Hind!

Title: Advocating for School Climate Resilience & Safety (NDMA SOP)
User_Story_x: On March 10, I, Lakshay Behal, attended the seminar "Sustainability Blueprint: Crafting a Better Future" at Doaba College Jalandhar , featuring guest speakers Ms. Meenal Verma (Managing Director, Green Brigade Pvt Ltd, Jalandhar) and Mr. Bharat Bansal (Community Manager, Reap Benefit). Meenal Ma’am spoke about common household waste, ways to dispose of, reuse, and recycle it, and explained how to make natural compost and bio-enzymes (used for washing vegetables) with the correct ratios. She also clarified which materials are recyclable and which are not. After that, Bharat Sir took the stage, starting with a short game to refresh our energy. He elaborated on Meenal Ma’am’s points and emphasized that we should apply what she taught to reduce waste in our daily lives. He then shared an inspiring incident about my friend Vabek, a Solve Ninja, who noticed a leaking pipe in our college. Vabek first talked to Bharat Sir, assuming that since he knew the principal, he would directly ask for the repair. However, Bharat Sir guided him to write a formal letter to the principal instead. After submitting it, the plumbers repaired the pipe the next day, which motivated Vabek. Bharat Sir then introduced Changemaker Profiles (CMP) and showcased both Vabek Sroya’s profile and my verified profile as examples. While talking about Changemaker Profiles (CMP), Bharat Sir also mentioned that Vabek and I were part of the core team that helped organize the event. He further highlighted our efforts in Jagraon, where we worked on fixing a black spot. When Bharat Sir shared this, Principal Sir and Dr. Ashwani Sir were impressed and praised our efforts. My friends and I also helped new students create their CMPs. At the end of the session, Bharat Sir asked everyone to join the group to stay connected and continue learning. Overall, it was an engaging and informative seminar filled with valuable learning experiences.

Title: Advocating for School Climate Resilience & Safety (NDMA SOP)
User_Story_x:
On January 22, 2025, I, Lakshay Behal from Doaba College Jalandhar, participated in a black spot cleaning initiative in front of Jagraon Bus Stand as part of Reap Benefit. Along with my teammate Vabek Sroya, we traveled from Jalandhar to Jagraon. A core team member guided us to the location, which was in terrible condition, with people continuing to dump garbage. When we stopped them, they felt guilty and promised not to do it again.

Soon, volunteers from Science College NSS, CT University, and municipal workers joined us. After receiving gloves, masks, and cleaning tools, we were divided into two teams—one for the cleanliness drive and the other for cleaning the black spot. Our team focused on clearing the garbage, collecting nearly 300 kg of waste, which was then disposed of in the municipal truck. After that, we leveled the area with soil brought by municipal workers.

While waiting, we had a delicious lunch of samosa, channa, and chai. Once the soil was settled, we painted the spot, which took about three hours. The municipal corporation president and executive officer honored us with certificates, and we received medals from Bharat Sir under his new initiative, Sanjhi Zimmedari. We then beautified the spot with chuna designs and set up chairs for a chai-mathi langar. I helped serve chai, and many locals appreciated our efforts.

After a successful day of teamwork and transformation, we left for Jalandhar. My favourite part of the event was painting and serving chai to the people of Jagraon, making it a truly memorable experience. 

Thank you, Bharat Sir, for the opportunity to step out of the city and be part of this impactful initiative!

You can watch our journey from here: https://www.youtube.com/shorts/kqioFo8vrfs.

Also on this :
https://youtu.be/6lezIbsDN4I?si=D9j1_Mi59gs13k5_

🟢 On 4th July 2025, I actively participated in the “Waste Work is Green Work” campaign as a campaigner under the weekly action shared by Reap Benefit. This campaign highlights the vital contributions of waste-pickers—especially women—in leading climate action through their daily efforts.

💚 In my post, I focused on the inspiring example of Pune, where over 3,850 women waste-pickers collect waste from 6 lakh+ homes, diverting 227 tonnes of waste daily, and helping to reduce nearly 1 lakh tonnes of CO₂ every year. I wanted to recognize them as climate heroes, not just workers, and encourage others to pay, equip, and respect them for their efforts.
📣 As part of my campaign action, I created a heartfelt LinkedIn post to raise awareness and amplify their voices. I used hashtags like #GreenWork, #WasteWarriors, and #OwnYourChange and included a call to action urging civic leaders and citizens to build inclusive systems that support waste-pickers with dignity and fairness.

🎉 A proud moment for me was when Reap Benefit officially reposted my LinkedIn campaign on their profile. This recognition gave a boost to my action and reaffirmed the importance of amplifying community-driven stories through social media platforms.

🌱 This campaign was not just a social media post, but a meaningful step toward dignity, justice, and climate awareness. I remain committed to supporting waste workers and driving change through continued community action.

Title: The 1-Meter Tree Freedom Audit & NGT Advocacy
User_Story_x: 🌱 On 📅 14 February 2025 I, along with Gurkamal, lakshay and friends, started the Plastic Files Replacement Project with the main objective of stopping the use of plastic files in college, especially for practicals. 🌱 Phase 1 – Starting & Planning continued on 📅 15 February 2025 when we held an online Google Meet where we discussed the harmful impact of plastic. Tasks were assigned to speak with HODs of different departments to understand what happens to plastic files after submission, keeping a record of everything. Gurkamal agreed to share a Google Form for the survey. 🌱 Phase 1 further continued on 📅 18–23 February 2025 when the survey form was prepared, modified with guidance from Bharat Sir, and then shared in Solve Squad groups. I, along with Lakshay, also met with our HOD, who suggested creating another survey for teachers. We followed up continuously to ensure circulation. 🌱 Phase 2 – Collecting Information began on 📅 5–11 March 2025 when I reminded HOD Sir again for survey circulation and also messaged students for filling responses. On 11 March, we held our second meet-up with Lakshay, Gurkamal, Prince, and Rahul. We discussed progress, set the target of collecting 100+ responses from each college by March 21, and encouraged one-on-one outreach with students. 🌱 Phase 2 continued on 📅 12–22 March 2025 as I actively shared the survey in groups and individually with students, getting responses day by day. HOD Sir also circulated it through teachers. By 20 March, I had personally shared with 31 students. Updates and summaries of responses were also shared in our project group and with the Punjab Core Team. 🌱 Phase 2 concluded on 📅 28–29 March 2025 when we compiled the survey summary with Gurkamal’s help, uploaded it on Google Docs, and shared it with Bharat Sir in PDF. Gurkamal also mailed it formally. 🌱 Phase 3 – Planning Action started on 📅 2–3 April 2025 when Bharat Sir scheduled a mentor call to discuss next steps. With Gurkamal, I finalized meeting details and later we reviewed survey findings, brainstormed action plans, and discussed strategies to engage faculty and students. 🌱 Phase 3 continued on 📅 5–9 April 2025 when lakshay shared project updates in the YICC Punjab community. We also informed Eco Club Coordinator Shivika Mam and HOD Sir about the survey results. Bharat Sir asked for next steps, and I outlined a plan: understanding the scrap dealer system, disposal process of files, identifying materials, and proposing eco-friendly alternatives like cardboard and college-branded files. Later, Lakshay and I confirmed with HOD that files go to regular scrap dealers, and I updated the Solve Ninja community about this. We also planned a small call with our team across universities. 🌱 Phase 3 concluded on 📅 10–17 April 2025 when we prepared the overall project report and had our third project meet-up where Gurkamal suggested drafting a letter to HODs with student signatures. I and Lakshay worked on letters, formatting, and collecting 33 student signatures. On 17 April, we submitted the letter to HOD Sir, who appreciated the initiative, assured that plastic files would be eliminated from next semester, and asked us to remind him. Updates were shared in Solve Ninja groups, and Bharat Sir suggested collecting photos of eco-friendly submissions. 🌱 Phase 4 – Taking Action started on 📅 29 April 2025 when Iakshay shared our project journey at the Learning by Doing Adda. 🌱 Phase 4 continued on 📅 1–6 August 2025 when I and Lakshay reminded HOD Sir about his promise to release a notice. He asked for a draft, which I prepared with inputs from Bharat Sir. After finalizing, I submitted it, and he mentioned escalating it to the college level. I bought and submitted sample files (plastic, cardboard, office types) as HOD Sir requested to show to Principal Sir. Updates were continuously shared with the core team. 🌱 Phase 4 further continued on 📅 11–14 August 2025 when I kept visiting HOD Sir to check on the notice. He mentioned he had signed it and needed to meet Principal Sir before releasing it. On 14 August, he assured me it would be released that day, considering upcoming holidays. 🌱 Phase 4 continued on 📅 18–30 August 2025 when HOD Sir informed me that he had submitted the notice to Principal Sir. On 30 August, I also met with Anil Sir, PA to Principal Sir, and requested him to check about the release. 🌱 Phase 5 – Growing Bigger began on 📅 8 September 2025 when along with Lakshay, I met Principal Sir, who told us he was considering introducing college-branded eco-friendly files included in fees for students, emphasizing the importance of alternatives. Later, we updated HOD Sir, who told us he had already instructed teachers not to accept plastic files and asked us to meet the Eco Club coordinator to expand this to the college level. I shared the update with the Punjab Core Team. ✅ Successfully completed the department-level Plastic Files Replacement Project. The campaign is now moving to Phase 6 – College Level Expansion — stay tuned!
User_Story_y:
Complexity: Medium | Time: 3 Weeks | Resources: Measuring tape, GPS app, camera, copy of NGT Order OA 82/2013.

THE STORY
I noticed that old-growth trees in my locality were being slowly killed by concrete pavements touching their trunks. I conducted a neighborhood audit to identify these violations and used the National Green Tribunal (NGT) mandates to petition the PWD for immediate de-concretization.

THE GOAL
To ensure every tree in the locality has the mandatory 1-meter soil radius for root aeration and water percolation.

THE STEPS
1. Census: Map every tree on your street. Measure the GBH (Girth at Breast Height) 1.3m from the ground.
2. Violation Audit: Use a tape to check if concrete/tiles are within 1m of the trunk (Violating NGT Case: Aditya N. Prasad vs. UoI).
3. Reporting: Log the exact Asset ID/Location on the municipal app under ‘Tree Health/Violation’.
4. Physical Action: Organize a ‘Manual De-Concretization’ day. Use hand-tools only (No JCBs) to remove surface tiles without damaging roots.

WHAT TO SAY
"Under NGT Order OA 82/2013, a 1-meter radius must be free of concrete. We have mapped 12 violations on [Road Name] and request immediate manual de-concretization to prevent tree-fall during storms."

OVERCOMING PROBLEMS
* The Problem: Contractors might argue that concrete is needed for ‘clean’ footpaths.
* The Fix: Show them Section 5 of the NGT order which explicitly prioritizes tree health over aesthetic tiling.

SUCCESS
Secured official contractor response for de-concretizing 8 major trees.
Gap_Analysis_y:
Specific Gap: Identified trees lack an official legal tag for digital reporting. Fix: Provide a Guide on Finding Asset IDs/Tags.
Specific Gap: Lacks a professional engineering report structure for the Tree Officer. Fix: Provide an official NGT-compliant Safety Audit Proforma.
Specific Gap: No formal baseline for community health perceptions. Fix: Add a 5-question Community Health Survey.
Specific Gap: Lacks a tracking mechanism for municipal follow-up. Fix: Provide a Civic Tracking Log template.
Admin_Fix_FullText: [TEMPLATE: TREE CENSUS] Columns: Species | GBH (cm) | Base Status (Soil/Cement) | Violation (Y/N). [LEGAL FIX: NGT OA 82/2013] Mandates 1m radius of soil around tree trunks.
Admin_Fix_Source: NGT OA 82/2013 / Aditya N. Prasad vs. Union of India & Ors.

Title: The 1-Meter Tree Freedom Audit & NGT Advocacy
User_Story_x: November 2019, I did my field work in government school Yavatmal so I have some idea about education in our society. As we can see that the number of children in municipal schools, government schools has reduced very much and schools are on the verge of complete closure. Even those families who are not financially well off are sending their children to private schools as the number of children in school has decreased as the teaching quality of municipal school teachers has also deteriorated. Therefore, I think that the number of children in municipal schools should increase, so that children who are not in good financial condition can also get a good quality education of their right. If municipal schools are closed, children whose situation is very dire. Those children will not be able to get an education. And if the municipal schools are closed, they will be deprived of education. Therefore, as a social work student, I think that we should think about this backward class in the society and prepare an action plan to increase the number of municipal schools and create awareness in the society. Field work should be given to the children working in fellowship in municipal schools from which the fellows will get the data of the drop out children and through their home visits they will counsel and try to bring the children to school. Through this initially 100% result will not be seen but 35% result will be seen and also we should work on finding the needy children in the society and focus on enrolling them in school and also try to get the educational materials they need through the government. Also, to bring quality in the education in the municipal corporation, we shall start an initiative to implement creative activity in the school by our fellows, which will make the children interested in school and if the parents see their response in studies is good, they will complete their further education in the municipal school itself. Because I am a girl studying in a municipal school living in Ner, a small taluka of Yavatmal district, but today I am studying in a place like Tata institute of social science Mumbai. So I think children can progress not only in private school but also in primary school as long as the quality of education is good.

Title: The 1-Meter Tree Freedom Audit & NGT Advocacy
User_Story_x:
Project Climate- READY (Climate Representation, Education & Action with Delhi Youth) aims to nurture Youth Environmental Champions across Delhi government schools through empowered eco- clubs, integrated with holistic climate education, increased youth participation and leadership in community-level interventions, and enhanced representation in the socio- environmental discourse at different levels. We have been working on the pilot by focussing on the pillars:
1. Creating & Strengthening Eco-Clubs: The project worked with Delhi government schools to identify existing eco-clubs or create new ones in schools that do not have them. 
2. Integrating Climate Education: The project integrated and delivered climate education into eco-club activities to enhance knowledge and awareness of environmental issues among students, by securing collaboration with 30+ organisations, subject matter experts and youth- led volunteer groups. 
3. Fostering Community Action & Leadership: The project empowered 400+ students to engage in community-based initiatives and awareness campaigns that engage students in practical activities and projects.
4. Identifying and Supporting Youth Environmental Champions: The project identified students who have a keen interest in environmental issues and ensured representation of these youth leaders by providing students to represent their school and communities at 5+ national and international level through collaborations with several organizations/ forums such as UNICEF India, UNESCO, LCOY India, POP Movement etc.

User_Story_x:
Here are some actions one can take to clean their surroundings:

1. Organized Cleanup Drives: Volunteered in neighborhood or community cleanup events to collect litter from streets, parks, or public areas.


2. Recycling Initiatives: Sorted waste into recyclables and non-recyclables at home and educated others about proper waste segregation.


3. Planting Trees: Planted trees or maintained a small garden to improve air quality and create a cleaner environment.


4. Proper Disposal of Waste: Ensured garbage was disposed of in designated bins and avoided littering in public places.


5. Awareness Campaigns: Conducted awareness sessions about maintaining cleanliness and reducing plastic use.


6. Adopted Sustainable Habits: Reduced the use of single-use plastics, carried reusable bags, and encouraged others to do the same.


7. Maintained Public Areas: Helped clean areas like bus stops, schools, or offices by removing dirt or clutter.


8. Community Collaboration: Collaborated with neighbors to establish regular cleanliness checks for common spaces.



These small yet impactful actions can inspire others to join in and contribute to a cleaner environment!

User_Story_x: In my role with the Plastic Usage Awareness Project, I spearheaded the creation and distribution of a comprehensive survey aimed at understanding daily plastic consumption, disposal habits, and awareness levels across diverse demographics. Utilizing my data orientation and critical thinking skills, I designed multiple-choice questions that effectively captured key insights, revealing prevalent use of plastic bags and bottles, low recycling rates, and limited awareness of environmental impacts like microplastics. The analysis indicated a strong willingness to adopt alternatives if accessible. Leveraging these insights, I orchestrated an awareness campaign targeting colleges and local communities, employing multilingual communication strategies (Kannada-English-Tamil) to ensure inclusivity and relatability. By incorporating real-life examples, such as the Kerala plastic pellet spill, I emphasized the urgency of the issue. Feedback was actively gathered to gauge public interest in sustainable practices. Ultimately, I compiled the data, analysis, and refined problem statement into a comprehensive report to bolster ongoing educational outreach efforts.

To ensure every tree in the locality has the mandatory 1-meter soil radius for root aeration and water percolation.

THE STEPS
1. Census: Map every tree on your street. Measure the GBH (Girth at Breast Height) 1.3m from the ground.
2. Violation Audit: Use a tape to check if concrete/tiles are within 1m of the trunk (Violating NGT Case: Aditya N. Prasad vs. UoI).
3. Reporting: Log the exact Asset ID/Location on the municipal app under ‘Tree Health/Violation’.
4. Physical Action: Organize a ‘Manual De-Concretization’ day. Use hand-tools only (No JCBs) to remove surface tiles without damaging roots.

WHAT TO SAY
"Under NGT Order OA 82/2013, a 1-meter radius must be free of concrete. We have mapped 12 violations on [Road Name] and request immediate manual de-concretization to prevent tree-fall during storms."

OVERCOMING PROBLEMS
* The Problem: Contractors might argue that concrete is needed for ‘clean’ footpaths.
* The Fix: Show them Section 5 of the NGT order which explicitly prioritizes tree health over aesthetic tiling.

SUCCESS
Secured official contractor response for de-concretizing 8 major trees.
Gap_Analysis_y:
Specific Gap: Identified trees lack an official legal tag for digital reporting. Fix: Provide a Guide on Finding Asset IDs/Tags.
Specific Gap: Lacks a professional engineering report structure for the Tree Officer. Fix: Provide an official NGT-compliant Safety Audit Proforma.
Specific Gap: No formal baseline for community health perceptions. Fix: Add a 5-question Community Health Survey.
Specific Gap: Lacks a tracking mechanism for municipal follow-up. Fix: Provide a Civic Tracking Log template.
Admin_Fix_FullText: [TEMPLATE: TREE CENSUS] Columns: Species | GBH (cm) | Base Status (Soil/Cement) | Violation (Y/N). [LEGAL FIX: NGT OA 82/2013] Mandates 1m radius of soil around tree trunks.
Admin_Fix_Source: NGT OA 82/2013 / Aditya N. Prasad vs. Union of India & Ors.

---

“Demotivation Didn’t Stop Us: Our Attempt for a Clean & Plastic-Free Event”

On 5th September, our village panchayat organized a tournament in our school where many children of Sadhanwala participated. Sadly, we could not make it a plastic-free event because permission was not given, and only disposable utensils were used. After the tournament, the panchayat did not take responsibility for cleaning the school, so we students decided to do it ourselves. Though the ground was closed that day, my teammate Harpreet Kaur showed great courage the next day. With two girls and her brother, she started cleaning the school, even when many people from the village tried to demotivate and scold her. Despite all the challenges, Harpreet did what she could, proving that true change makers never give up. Even though we could not fully succeed, our efforts to keep the school clean and plastic-free will always inspire us to try again.


---

Title: Neighborhood Walkability & IRC-103 Footpath Audit
User_Story_x:
I had the privilege of serving as a Campus Director for the Millennium Fellowship 2024, an initiative by United Nations Academic Impact (UNAI) and the Millennium Campus Network (MCN). This global fellowship is a prestigious leadership program that brings together young changemakers committed to advancing the UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).

As Campus Director, I led a cohort of 8-26 Fellows at National Institute of Technology Tiruchirappalli (NIT Trichy). My responsibilities included coordinating weekly sessions, mentoring Fellows, and fostering collaboration among emerging leaders. I also served as the liaison between the global MCN team and my cohort, ensuring that our projects were aligned with the SDGs and that each Fellow received the support they needed to drive change in their communities.

The Letter of Acknowledgement from MCN recognizes my role in strategic leadership, mentorship, and project facilitation. It highlights my ability to balance planning with empathy, ensuring every Fellow felt supported while tackling critical local issues. My work through the Millennium Fellowship not only advanced our cohort’s goals but also strengthened my commitment to ethical leadership and inclusive social impact.

Millennium Fellowship Letter of Acknowledgement -  [https://drive.google.com/file/d/1GmWcTY3KCy-wjX0Viu0R-FZgVO4mpkq2/view?usp=sharing]
Millennium Fellowship Certificate -  [https://drive.google.com/file/d/1D2gpb9yXrGv77j11TObGi912agE0FQuF/view?usp=sharing]
User_Story_y:
Complexity: Medium | Time: 2 Weeks | Resources: Measuring tape, smartphone camera, Lux Meter app, copy of IRC:103-2012.

THE STORY
I audited the walkability of my neighborhood streets to ensure they meet the Indian Road Congress (IRC) standards. I focused on identifying obstructions like broken pavers, illegal parking, and ‘dark-spots’ that make walking unsafe for women, children, and the elderly.

THE GOAL
To achieve a continuous, unobstructed pedestrian clear zone of at least 1.8m width in residential blocks.

THE STEPS
1. Clear Zone Audit: Measure the width of the footpath. Per IRC:103, it must be at least 1.8m. Mark any point where it drops below this.
2. Obstruction Logging: Map any ‘Fixed Obstructions’ (Poles, Transformers, Dustbins) that should be moved to the ‘Furniture Zone’.
3. Night Audit: Use a Lux Meter app to check if lighting is between 25-30 lux. Identify ‘Dark Spots’ for new streetlight poles.
4. Official Filing: Submit the ‘Street Audit Report’ to the PWD/Municipal Executive Engineer using the ward-level 311 app.

WHAT TO SAY
"Our audit of [Street Name] shows it fails to meet the IRC:103-2012 guideline, as the clear walking path is only 0.8m wide due to [Obstruction]. We request a redesign of the footpath to ensure 1.8m of continuous mobility."

OVERCOMING PROBLEMS
* The Problem: Shopkeepers or residents might view the ‘Furniture Zone’ as space for their own extended use.
* The Fix: Explain that clear footpaths reduce ‘walking-in-traffic’ and actually improve safety and visibility for local businesses.

SUCCESS
Presented the audit to the Ward Committee, resulting in a formal work order for clearing 3 major footpath obstructions.
Gap_Analysis_y:
Specific Gap: The audit lacks a formal ‘Universal Accessibility’ check for persons with disabilities (e.g. tactile pavers/ramps). Fix: Provide a 4-point ADA-compliance checklist for ramps.
Specific Gap: No direct linkage between street-lighting levels and reported feelings of safety. Fix: Add a ‘Night-Safety Perception Survey’ for 10 residents.
Specific Gap: Lacks a technical audit of footpath-to-road height (Target: max 150mm). Fix: Add a standard Curb-Height Measurement guide.
Specific Gap: No tracking of municipal resolve time once reported. Fix: Provide a Civic Resolve Tracker.
Admin_Fix_Summary: Urban Biodiversity & Heritage Tree Protection SOP
Admin_Fix_FullText: [TEMPLATE: FOOTPATH AUDIT] Columns: Location | Measured Width (m) | Paving Quality | Lighting (Lux). [LEGAL FIX: IRC:103-2012] Specifies the minimum clear width of 1.8m for pedestrian paths.
Admin_Fix_Source: IRC:103-2012 (Guidelines for Pedestrian Facilities) / Street Design SOPs
