The local people took the initiative to preserve old heritage trees by surveying and mapping them. Trees are invaluable to the ecosystem. They not only provide resources to the people but also help mitigate carbon dioxide which is essential for climate action.
The mapped location marks the area of trees surveyed by a volunteer in their neighborhood who has contributed to India's first Green Cover Database.

Click on the following link to lean more about the value of trees:
https://tinyurl.com/2sashxsd
Why geotagging trees is important and why should it be made public:
https://tinyurl.com/54p3k8kvMapped Trees in my Locality and Contributed to India's First #GreenCover Database
User_Story_y:
Complexity: Medium | Time: 2 Weeks | Resources: Measuring tape, smartphone camera, Lux Meter app, copy of IRC:103-2012.

THE STORY
I audited the walkability of my neighborhood streets to ensure they meet the Indian Road Congress (IRC) standards. I focused on identifying obstructions like broken pavers, illegal parking, and ‘dark-spots’ that make walking unsafe for women, children, and the elderly.

THE GOAL
To achieve a continuous, unobstructed pedestrian clear zone of at least 1.8m width in residential blocks.

THE STEPS
1. Clear Zone Audit: Measure the width of the footpath. Per IRC:103, it must be at least 1.8m. Mark any point where it drops below this.
2. Obstruction Logging: Map any ‘Fixed Obstructions’ (Poles, Transformers, Dustbins) that should be moved to the ‘Furniture Zone’.
3. Night Audit: Use a Lux Meter app to check if lighting is between 25-30 lux. Identify ‘Dark Spots’ for new streetlight poles.
4. Official Filing: Submit the ‘Street Audit Report’ to the PWD/Municipal Executive Engineer using the ward-level 311 app.

WHAT TO SAY
"Our audit of [Street Name] shows it fails to meet the IRC:103-2012 guideline, as the clear walking path is only 0.8m wide due to [Obstruction]. We request a redesign of the footpath to ensure 1.8m of continuous mobility."

OVERCOMING PROBLEMS
* The Problem: Shopkeepers or residents might view the ‘Furniture Zone’ as space for their own extended use.
* The Fix: Explain that clear footpaths reduce ‘walking-in-traffic’ and actually improve safety and visibility for local businesses.

SUCCESS
Presented the audit to the Ward Committee, resulting in a formal work order for clearing 3 major footpath obstructions.
Gap_Analysis_y:
Specific Gap: The audit lacks a formal ‘Universal Accessibility’ check for persons with disabilities (e.g. tactile pavers/ramps). Fix: Provide a 4-point ADA-compliance checklist for ramps.
Specific Gap: No direct linkage between street-lighting levels and reported feelings of safety. Fix: Add a ‘Night-Safety Perception Survey’ for 10 residents.
Specific Gap: Lacks a technical audit of footpath-to-road height (Target: max 150mm). Fix: Add a standard Curb-Height Measurement guide.
Specific Gap: No tracking of municipal resolve time once reported. Fix: Provide a Civic Resolve Tracker.
Admin_Fix_Summary: Urban Biodiversity & Heritage Tree Protection SOP
Admin_Fix_FullText: [TEMPLATE: FOOTPATH AUDIT] Columns: Location | Measured Width (m) | Paving Quality | Lighting (Lux). [LEGAL FIX: IRC:103-2012] Specifies the minimum clear width of 1.8m for pedestrian paths.
Admin_Fix_Source: IRC:103-2012 (Guidelines for Pedestrian Facilities) / Street Design SOPs

Title: Leading Neighborhood Change through Ward Committee Advocacy
User_Story_x:
Solverjam First Meetup(10th May 2025):-
Recently I attended the first session of SolverJam: Solving Problems with Tech and AI. It is part of a 6-week program called Digital Tools for Social Change: Problem-Solving with Tech & The goal of this program is to help us learn how to solve real-world problems using technology and artificial intelligence. The session was hosted by Gautamraj Sir and supported by Chiku Sir and Navneet Ma’am. The meeting began with Chiku Sir explaining the agenda. After that, we moved into breakout rooms where we introduced ourselves by sharing our names, the meanings of our names, and whether we had heard about or used AI before. Once we came back from the breakout rooms, Gautamraj Sir led an interesting activity where we had to guess if a picture was made by AI or a real one. After this, we had a Learning by Doing session called Tech Mapping. In this activity, we drew a rough outline of our house on a piece of paper. Then, we walked around our house to identify two most used and two least used tech or non-tech items. We also listed the top 5 mobile apps used in our homes. Later, we shared our tech maps and thoughts with our teammates in breakout rooms. It was fun and helped us understand how much we use different tools in daily life.



👉👉Week 1 Challenge(14th May 2025):-
After the session, we were asked to download Google Classroom. We also received a link to join the classroom for future assignments and updates. We then got our Week 1 Challenge. The theme is How is technology changing your street? For this, we have to observe, collect data, and talk to people in our area. Before starting the Tech Walk, we also spent time preparing our survey questions to make sure we asked the right things and collected useful information. I successfully completed and submitted my assignment on 14th May 2025, where I collaborated with my peers Lakshay and Armaan to conduct a Tech Walk near Doaba College. We interacted with local vendors including a Parshad Shop inside Devi Talab Mandir, Dr. Deo’z Pizza & Cafe, and a tea stall near Kamal Hospital. Through these surveys, we observed different levels of technology use—ranging from CCTV cameras, QR payments, and biometric systems to resistance due to language barriers or banking issues. The insights highlighted how tech adoption varies based on need, awareness, and accessibility. 

👉👉Mentoring Clinic (15th May 2025):-
After that i also participated in the Mentoring Clinic session on Zoom, hosted by Chiku Sir, Gautamraj Sir, and Navneet Ma’am. The session began with an introduction activity in the chat, followed by sharing our biggest question for the mentors. We then visualized two scenarios: a day with everything using technology and a day without any technology, and shared two related images. Participants also created and discussed images generated using AI tools. After that, we were divided into breakout rooms where we reflected on our observations and findings from the Week 1 challenge. Upon returning to the main room, one representative from each breakout room shared the key points discussed. The session ended with a group photo to capture the moment. Week 1 went amazing looking forward to week 2 challenge.
User_Story_y:
Complexity: High | Time: 4 Weeks | Resources: Ward Committee meeting schedule, 74th CA summary, list of 10 community signatures.

THE STORY
I spearheaded a campaign to institutionalize youth participation in our local ward committee. By mapping community issues—from waste blackspots to poor lighting—I demonstrated the power of collective data to influence the annual ward-level budgetary priorities.

THE GOAL
To secure a recurring seat for youth observers in the monthly Ward Committee meetings.

THE STEPS
1. Issue Mapping: Spend 7 days collecting 10 unique civic complaints from your immediate neighbors.
2. Stakeholder Coalition: Get 10 signatures from residents supporting the ‘Youth Civic Observation’ initiative.
3. Meeting Prep: Draft a 1-page ‘Neighborhood Civic Status Report’ based on your collected data.
4. Formal Entry: Request a meeting with the Ward Councilor to present your report and ask for observer status in the next committee meeting.

WHAT TO SAY
"Under the 74th Constitutional Amendment, citizen participation in Ward Committees is a right. We have data on 10 critical issues in our block and we wish to support the committee as a Youth Data Partner."

OVERCOMING PROBLEMS
* The Problem: The Ward Councilor might dismiss the youth as ‘students’ without a stake in governance.
* The Fix: Show them your ‘Data Report.’ Politicians value accurate, ground-level information that helps them fulfill their mandates.

SUCCESS
The Ward Committee officially invited two youth members to observe and present data at their next monthly session.
Gap_Analysis_y:
Specific Gap: The campaign lacks a formal awareness of the 74th Constitutional Amendment’s decentralization mandates. Fix: Provide a 1-page summary of the 74th CA for youth.
Specific Gap: No structured template for a ‘Neighborhood Civic Status Report.’ Fix: Provide a Data Visualization template for civic issues.
Specific Gap: Lacks a formal follow-up mechanism for verbal promises made by officials. Fix: Provide a ‘Meeting Minutes’ and ‘Action Taken’ tracker.
Specific Gap: No linkage to local NGO advocacy networks. Fix: Provide a directory of local civic NGOs.
Admin_Fix_FullText: [TEMPLATE: CIVIC STATUS REPORT] Category | Count | Status | Required Action. [LEGAL FIX: 74th CA] Empowers citizens to participate in local urban governance via Ward Committees.
Admin_Fix_Source: 74th Constitutional Amendment Act / Urban Governance SOPs

Title: Leading Neighborhood Change through Ward Committee Advocacy
User_Story_x:
As part of the Water Audit, I conducted an observation of the water situation in my house and community. The primary source of water in our area is Samarsebel, a local water supply system. Water is stored in water tanks in homes, which ensures a consistent and accessible supply for daily use.

In my house, there are 25 taps installed, and the water is used for multiple purposes including drinking, cooking, washing utensils, bathing, washing clothes, watering plants, and other daily activities. To ensure safe consumption, we have a water purifier installed in our house, so the drinking water is clean and purified. We do not boil the water or use bleaching powder. The colour of the water is blue, indicating clarity and cleanliness. On visual inspection, there are no visible particles in the drinking water, and it is considered safe to consume.

Regarding water management infrastructure, there is proper drainage in our area, and no water leakage is observed from taps or hand pumps. Additionally, no water wastage has been reported, and no waste is thrown into water bodies, which helps maintain cleanliness and hygiene in the locality.

Access to water is not a challenge in our community. Everyone has access to drinking water, and no one needs to travel to fetch it. The availability of taps, clean storage, and proper purification systems reflect a well-managed water system in our locality.

This audit highlights that our community is currently practicing responsible water usage and management. However, continued awareness and conservation efforts are essential to sustain this good condition. Encouraging water-saving habits, monitoring leakage regularly, and educating others about clean water practices can further strengthen our community’s approach to sustainable water use.

Demographics: People of different age and cultural backgrounds reside here 

Socio-economic Status: Discuss the economic diversity, from affluent professionals to working-class families. "It's a mixed-income area, with upscale residences alongside more affordable housing options."

Cultural Background: Mention the cultural diversity and how it influences the community. "The neighborhood boasts a rich cultural tapestry, with significant contributions from various ethnic groups such as Asian, African-American, and European."

Occupational Diversity: Highlight the types of jobs and industries represented. "Professionals in finance and technology sectors are common here, along with a thriving community of artists and small business owners."

Lifestyle and Interests: Describe common interests or activities that bind the community together. "Residents are often seen enjoying outdoor activities like jogging and biking in the local parks, reflecting a health-conscious lifestyle."

Community Spirit: Mention any community initiatives or events that bring people together. "There's a strong sense of community, with regular events like street fairs and neighborhood clean-ups organized by local associations."

Educational and Cultural Institutions: Note the presence of schools, libraries, and cultural centers that shape the area. "The locality is well-served by top-rated schools and a public library known for its extensive collection and community programs."

By weaving these elements together, you can paint a comprehensive picture of the types of people who live in a particular locality, capturing both the diversity and unique characteristics that define the community.

User_Story_y:
Complexity: High | Time: 4 Weeks | Resources: Ward Committee meeting schedule, 74th CA summary, list of 10 community signatures.

THE STORY
I spearheaded a campaign to institutionalize youth participation in our local ward committee. By mapping community issues—from waste blackspots to poor lighting—I demonstrated the power of collective data to influence the annual ward-level budgetary priorities.

THE GOAL
To secure a recurring seat for youth observers in the monthly Ward Committee meetings.

THE STEPS
1. Issue Mapping: Spend 7 days collecting 10 unique civic complaints from your immediate neighbors.
2. Stakeholder Coalition: Get 10 signatures from residents supporting the ‘Youth Civic Observation’ initiative.
3. Meeting Prep: Draft a 1-page ‘Neighborhood Civic Status Report’ based on your collected data.
4. Formal Entry: Request a meeting with the Ward Councilor to present your report and ask for observer status in the next committee meeting.

WHAT TO SAY
"Under the 74th Constitutional Amendment, citizen participation in Ward Committees is a right. We have data on 10 critical issues in our block and we wish to support the committee as a Youth Data Partner."

OVERCOMING PROBLEMS
* The Problem: The Ward Councilor might dismiss the youth as ‘students’ without a stake in governance.
* The Fix: Show them your ‘Data Report.’ Politicians value accurate, ground-level information that helps them fulfill their mandates.

SUCCESS
The Ward Committee officially invited two youth members to observe and present data at their next monthly session.
Gap_Analysis_y:
Specific Gap: The campaign lacks a formal awareness of the 74th Constitutional Amendment’s decentralization mandates. Fix: Provide a 1-page summary of the 74th CA for youth.
Specific Gap: No structured template for a ‘Neighborhood Civic Status Report.’ Fix: Provide a Data Visualization template for civic issues.
Specific Gap: Lacks a formal follow-up mechanism for verbal promises made by officials. Fix: Provide a ‘Meeting Minutes’ and ‘Action Taken’ tracker.
Specific Gap: No linkage to local NGO advocacy networks. Fix: Provide a directory of local civic NGOs.

Title: Leading Neighborhood Change through Ward Committee Advocacy
User_Story_x:
Street audit was conducted at various locations across the country. The audit takes about 15 mins to complete and is done through a WhatsApp-based chatbot. The tool enables citizens to identify issues in their streets, understand the problems and investigate by collecting hyper-local data.

Here is the summary of my street: The street is located in a residential area with 50-100 houses and is generally in good condition with no potholes and a functional footpath. However, there are vehicles parked on the footpath or sides, which may obstruct pedestrian movement. The streetlights are not in good condition, which could pose a safety risk at night, although the signboards are adequate. There are two electrical transformers without protective grills, which could be a safety hazard. While the street does not flood during heavy rains and there is no stagnant water, there are blackspots present, and waste segregation does not occur at home, though waste collection happens daily. Occasionally, there are issues with street rodents, but there are no problems with stray dogs or open defecation. Overall, the street has some infrastructure and hygiene issues that need addressing, particularly concerning streetlights, waste management, and safety around electrical transformers.

Title: SBM-U 2.0: Community Toilet ODF+ Maintenance Audit
User_Story_x:
📅 On 29 June 2025, I officially kickstarted planning for Solver Jam – Moga by sending a message in the Punjab Solve Ninja Core Team group. After the successful organization of Solver Jam – Jalandhar, we looked forward to expanding the impact to Moga. I proposed 4 potential dates in July and created a poll to finalize the date. This was followed by constant reminders and one-on-one follow-ups to ensure maximum team participation. 🗳️
📞 From 1st to 4th July, I sent reminder messages to those who hadn’t voted in the poll and individually followed up with team members like Vabek, Rahul, Riya Mam, and Kiran. I helped finalize 20th July (Sunday) based on team availability. Rahul confirmed he would be in Moga after the 16th, while Kiran informed she wouldn’t be available. I kept mentors updated with confirmations. ✅
🧭 On 5th July, I asked Bharat Sir to guide us on the next steps now that the date was locked. He suggested having a team meeting soon. On 10 July, Bharat Sir shared the two key planning areas: Outreach and Topic finalization. I suggested practical, hands-on topics like Street Audit, Water Audit, and Garbage Spot Fixing, and gave input on integrating real-life skills like communication and critical thinking through fun civic actions. Kuldeep Sir appreciated this approach and emphasized embedding learning through climate and civic issues. 💬
📍Meanwhile, two venue options in Moga were explored — Nachhatar Bhawan and Kashmiri Park — and by 15 July, the final venue was fixed: Shaheed Comrade Nachhattar Singh Dhaliwal Bhawan near the Moga Bus Stand. The session title was confirmed as “Think like an AI, Ask like a Changemaker”. I verified the registration link, helped test Punjabi language flow, and prepared to share outreach posts. 📌
🚀 Between 16 and 18 July, I worked on outreach. I shared the poster and invite message (in both English and Punjabi) along with the registration link in Jalandhar, Ludhiana, and Patiala Solve Squad groups. I also created a poll to track who registered and followed up personally with interested participants like Rajan Preet Singh Bains, who responded positively. I sent updates to the core team and coordinated confirmations. 📲
🚌 By 18-19 July, I created a temporary travel coordination group (Moga Travel Temp) to manage Jalandhar participants’ travel. I added Palak, Arpana, Kajal, Kanchan, Vabek, Jyoti, Tulsi, and Mehak, and discussed meetup timing. I verified the earliest bus from Jalandhar (7:43 AM) and informed everyone to reach the bus stand by 7:15 AM sharp. Bharat Sir asked me to follow up with participants like Gurdeep, Dinesh, Akash, and Roji, which I did via calls and messages. 📞
📋 I created and shared a final confirmation poll, confirmed that Jyoti and her friends needed to register, and followed up with Kanchan to ensure they do so. I took responsibility to ensure smooth coordination and check-ins, even responding to participant queries about travel and logistics. Bharat Sir guided me throughout and appreciated the updates. 📝
🌄 On 20 July (Event Day), I coordinated the Jalandhar team’s travel. I reached the bus stand by 7:00 AM and helped Kajal, Vabek, Kanchan, Jyoti, Mehak, and Tulsi board the PRTC bus, and we departed around 7:30 AM. I informed the core team about our status and stayed connected throughout. 🚌
🎉 The Solver Jam – Moga started at 10:30 AM and was hosted by Bharat Sir. The session began with an icebreaker round where participants shared their name, city, feelings, and why they came. The first activity was a pattern game, starting with the word “class” — I broke the pattern with the word shararte (naughtiness), which helped demonstrate the concept of pattern breaking. We repeated the game with the word “waste”. 🎲

🤖 After the icebreaker and pattern-breaking game, Bharat Sir engaged us in a discussion about the AI tools we commonly use. Participants shared names like ChatGPT, Gemini, Midjourney, and others. This led into the next phase — a series of group activities to understand how AI works behind the scenes. Bharat Sir divided all participants into 5 teams of 4 members each. He then explained the five major components of AI: Prompt, Meaning Maker, Language Maker, Bias Checker, and Safety Checker.
We moved into our first group activity, where each team acted out one of these components. Bharat Sir gave us a controversial and thought-provoking prompt: “Are girls better at driving than boys?” Each group had to respond from the perspective of their assigned component. For example, the Bias Checker identified stereotypes and societal biases, the Language Maker restructured the sentence, and the Meaning Maker interpreted the context. This activity helped us realize how complex decisions and responses are made by AI systems. 🧩
🛠️ The second group activity built on the first. Bharat Sir gave us a vague prompt and asked each group to refine and improve it so that an AI would give a clearer and more useful response. This exercise taught us how to craft effective prompts and the importance of clarity, specificity, and intent in communication with AI tools. The entire session was not only fun and engaging but also sharpened our critical thinking and AI literacy. 🧠
🍽️ We then took a refreshment break with bananas, Tang, patties, and sandwiches. After the break, a new activity focused on identifying if a sentence was real but vague, wrong ,completely false, or helpful, strengthening our judgment and clarity skills. The last segment was a three-part reflection on: What we learned, how we would ask better prompts, and our favorite part of the event. 📚
📸 The event ended with a group photo and good energy. It was a powerful and joyful experience where I got to interact with new people, sharpen communication, and witness learning through action. I reached home around 7 PM, feeling fulfilled and proud of our collective effort. 🙌
User_Story_y:
Complexity: Medium | Time: 2 Weeks | Resources: Sanitation audit log, soap, water-testing kit, copy of SBM-U 2.0 ODF+ protocol.

THE STORY
I audited the maintenance standards of our local community toilet blocks to ensure they comply with the Swachh Bharat Mission-Urban (SBM-U) 2.0 ODF+ requirements. I focused on identifying non-functional lighting, lack of soap, and stagnant water issues that impact community health and dignity.

THE GOAL
To ensure the ULB maintains 100% functionality and hygiene standards per the ODF+ certification protocol.

THE STEPS
1. Functional Check: Audit the toilet block using the ODF+ checklist: Check for constant water, functional flush, and working lights/locks.
2. Consumable Audit: Check for soap availability and hygienic waste bins. Note if the 5-year O&M contract details are displayed.
3. User Survey: Interview 5 regular users about their experience with the onsite caretaker and cleanliness.
4. Official Filing: Submit the ‘Sanitation Audit Report’ to the ULB Sanitary Inspector or use the SBM Swachhata app for photo-logging issues.

WHAT TO SAY
"Under the SBM-U 2.0 ODF+ Protocol, community toilets must have 24/7 water availability and functional lighting. Our audit of CT #[Number] shows [Issue]. We request immediate rectification to maintain our city’s status."

OVERCOMING PROBLEMS
* The Problem: The caretaker might worry that the audit is an attempt to get them fired.
* The Fix: Explain that the audit helps the ULB understand the resources they need (more soap, better bulbs) to do their job well.

SUCCESS
The ULB responded within 48 hours to fix the lighting and restock consumables at the audited toilet block.
Gap_Analysis_y:
Specific Gap: The audit lacks a formal check of Faecal Sludge & Septage Management (FSSM) compliance. Fix: Add a check for septic tank cleaning frequency logs.
Specific Gap: No direct linkage between toilet conditions and school/work attendance for girls. Fix: Add a 3-question survey on ‘Sanitation & Access’.
Specific Gap: Lacks a technical check for disability-friendly features (e.g. ramp slope, grab bars). Fix: Provide a 4-point ADA-compliance checklist.
Specific Gap: No tracking of the 5-year O&M contract compliance. Fix: Provide a ‘Contract Tracking Sheet’ for community members.
Admin_Fix_FullText: [TEMPLATE: ODF+ AUDIT] Columns: Facility ID | Water (Y/N) | Lighting (Y/N) | Soap (Y/N) | Odor Level. [LEGAL FIX: SBM-U 2.0 Protocol] Mandates 100% functionality for ODF+ certification.
Admin_Fix_Source: SBM-Urban 2.0 ODF+ Protocol / ULB Sanitation SOPs

Title: SBM-U 2.0: Community Toilet ODF+ Maintenance Audit
User_Story_x:
📅 On 20 September 2025, I, along with my friend Vabek Sroya, met Shivika Ma’am. She informed us that we were selected to present in the upcoming orientation session. She also instructed us to prepare well and get the presentation checked on Monday.
📅 On 22 September 2025, we prepared a presentation and met Shivika Ma’am and Bharat Bansal Sir, along with Vabek. We presented our work, but Ma’am said more preparation was needed. I told her that I would also prepare detailed written material, and she agreed to meet me again the next day for review. By this stage, our presentation was prepared ✅ and the material was also written ✅.
📅 On 23 September 2025, we again met Shivika Ma’am and presented our prepared presentation. She gave us a few suggestions: Vabek should start by introducing the Eco Club, we should follow the planned flow throughout, and we should also include the opportunities we got through the club.
📅 On 25 September 2025, I worked on the updated presentation by incorporating Ma’am’s suggestions. Later, we again met her and presented the revised version for feedback.
🌱 Finally, on 26 September 2025, I and my friend Vabek gave our presentation on our journey and the work we have done so far. The event began with a welcome speech by Ashvini Sir (Eco Club Coordinator). After this, Ashvini Sir, Shivika Ma’am, and Bharat Sir welcomed Mr. Kuldeep Dantewadia (CEO, Reap Benefit) with a sapling.
🎤 Then Rajni Ma’am invited us on the dais. Vabek began by speaking about what the Eco Club is, how it was formed, and its purpose. After that, he invited me, and I shared my experience of fixing the garbage spot opposite Kamal Hospital. Then Vabek spoke about the garbage spot opposite Devi Talab Mandir, and I explained how we ensured long-term cleanliness there through proper cementing.
🚮 Next, Vabek described how we got the opportunity to visit Jagraon, where we cleaned a black spot, removed more than 300 kg of garbage, and transformed it into a parking space. Since most of these events were initiated by others, he also explained how we ourselves started the plastic file project. We wrote a letter to our HOD Sir, proposing to use cardboard files instead of plastic ones, as plastic files were not being recycled properly. After our submission, HOD Sir issued a notice in all department groups.
🌍 Vabek further highlighted that our efforts were not limited to garbage spot cleanups. We also took part in the 30-Day Environmental Challenge, where every day we received a task to complete and share with a geo-tagged photo. He also shared about our participation in plantation and cleanliness drives.
📢 After this, I spoke about NYCC (National Youth Climate Consortium), a youth-led initiative by the Bring Back Green Foundation, supported by YuWaah at UNICEF. I explained my selection journey: from filling the application form to the project round, interview, project proposal, and finally attending a 4-day training in Dehradun. I also shared about the stakeholders supporting my project, including the Eco Club Coordinator, NSS Coordinator, HOD of Computer Science & IT, Health Officer of Municipal Corporation Jalandhar, and Sanitary Inspector Vikrant Sir. Their guidance has been very valuable. Then I explained my vision: to fix garbage spots across Jalandhar, build a community of like-minded youth, and inspire others to take action. I also shared about the Pathankot Chownk garbage spot (near Bijli Daftar) where I have already started working and my future plans.
🙌 At the end, Vabek gave the closing remarks by narrating how he fixed a water pipe leakage through a letter to Principal Sir. He encouraged students not to ignore problems but to take action.
✨ After our presentation, Bharat Sir spoke about the Change Maker Profile, and then Kuldeep Sir addressed the gathering. The event concluded with the badge distribution ceremony, where I was given the post of President and Vabek was given the post of Vice President. The newly formed Cleanliness Squad members also received their badges.
User_Story_y:
Complexity: Medium | Time: 2 Weeks | Resources: Sanitation audit log, soap, water-testing kit, copy of SBM-U 2.0 ODF+ protocol.

THE STORY
I audited the maintenance standards of our local community toilet blocks to ensure they comply with the Swachh Bharat Mission-Urban (SBM-U) 2.0 ODF+ requirements. I focused on identifying non-functional lighting, lack of soap, and stagnant water issues that impact community health and dignity.

THE GOAL
To ensure the ULB maintains 100% functionality and hygiene standards per the ODF+ certification protocol.

THE STEPS
1. Functional Check: Audit the toilet block using the ODF+ checklist: Check for constant water, functional flush, and working lights/locks.
2. Consumable Audit: Check for soap availability and hygienic waste bins. Note if the 5-year O&M contract details are displayed.
3. User Survey: Interview 5 regular users about their experience with the onsite caretaker and cleanliness.
4. Official Filing: Submit the ‘Sanitation Audit Report’ to the ULB Sanitary Inspector or use the SBM Swachhata app for photo-logging issues.

WHAT TO SAY
"Under the SBM-U 2.0 ODF+ Protocol, community toilets must have 24/7 water availability and functional lighting. Our audit of CT #[Number] shows [Issue]. We request immediate rectification to maintain our city’s status."

OVERCOMING PROBLEMS
* The Problem: The caretaker might worry that the audit is an attempt to get them fired.
* The Fix: Explain that the audit helps the ULB understand the resources they need (more soap, better bulbs) to do their job well.

SUCCESS
The ULB responded within 48 hours to fix the lighting and restock consumables at the audited toilet block.
Gap_Analysis_y:
Specific Gap: The audit lacks a formal check of Faecal Sludge & Septage Management (FSSM) compliance. Fix: Add a check for septic tank cleaning frequency logs.
Specific Gap: No direct linkage between toilet conditions and school/work attendance for girls. Fix: Add a 3-question survey on ‘Sanitation & Access’.
Specific Gap: Lacks a technical check for disability-friendly features (e.g. ramp slope, grab bars). Fix: Provide a 4-point ADA-compliance checklist.
Specific Gap: No tracking of the 5-year O&M contract compliance. Fix: Provide a ‘Contract Tracking Sheet’ for community members.

Title: SBM-U 2.0: Community Toilet ODF+ Maintenance Audit
User_Story_x:
During Week 2 of the SolverJam program, we received our second weekly challenge with the theme “Leveraging Tech to Represent Problems Around Me.” This assignment, scheduled for submission between May 17 and May 24, 2025, required us to select a real-world issue from our community, understand it thoroughly, and use simple technology tools to clearly represent and communicate the problem.

I worked in a team with Vabek, who collaborated with me at Doaba College, Jalandhar, while Gurkamal contributed from CT University, Ludhiana. Together, we chose to investigate the environmental impact of plastic stick files used during practical exams at our colleges. These single-use plastic files generate considerable waste and contribute to pollution after exam periods.

We gathered information through observations and conversations with students and faculty to draft a clear and simple problem statement. We also practiced explaining the issue in easy-to-understand language for children and elders, ensuring effective communication to different audiences.

We completed and submitted the assignment on May 21, 2025. This task helped us improve our problem identification skills and use technology thoughtfully to represent community issues, building a strong foundation for the weeks ahead.

🎯👉On 22nd May 2025, I participated in Mentoring Clinic-2, hosted by Chiku Sir and Gautamraj Sir. The session began with an activity titled "Use AI to Identify Problems" where we were asked to use any AI tool (like ChatGPT, Gemini, or Claude), take a picture from our surroundings using the camera option, and ask the AI to identify possible problems visible in the image. This helped us practice using AI creatively for real-world observations.
After this, we were moved into breakout rooms, where each participant shared their top 3 understandings or insights from the assignment and the AI activity. Once we returned to the main room, one representative from each group shared the key points discussed in their respective breakout rooms, helping everyone learn from each other's experiences.
Following this, participants asked questions to Chiku Sir and Gautamraj Sir, who gave thoughtful answers and advice based on our findings. The session concluded with a group photo, capturing our continued learning journey together.
This week helped strengthen my skills in identifying problems, using digital tools for representation, and collaborating with AI for creative solutions. I’m excited for what’s next in the upcoming weeks of SolverJam.

🎯👉On 24th May 2025, I participated in Peer Adda-1, where we were divided into teams based on language preference. I joined the Hindi-speaking team and was randomly grouped with teammates, and together we named our team Team Aasmaan. After introductions, each member shared a personal issue related to climate or citizen problems. 

Our team chose to address the problem of potholes on roads in places like Punjab and Assam, which lead to traffic jams, accidents, and daily inconvenience. We conducted role-play interviews using ChatGPT and mentors, applied the 5 Whys technique to explore root causes, and created a clear problem statement in Hindi. 


We then brainstormed solutions like regular road maintenance, forming a WhatsApp group for alerts, drainage improvements, CCTV installation, and a Road Solve Squad. 

For our prototype, we proposed a digital campaign called Solve Squad, where citizens can send pothole photos via Instagram and get guidance. A WhatsApp bot would also help users take action. 

Our efforts paid off—we won the Peer Adda Jam for the fastest prototyping, which was a proud moment for our team. 

The session concluded with a joyful group photo, marking another step in our collaborative learning journey.


The week 2  ended with reflections on teamwork and unexpected learnings, making it a valuable part of our Week 2 journey.

Title: SBM-U 2.0: Community Toilet ODF+ Maintenance Audit
User_Story_x:
I Lakshay Behal attended the first session of SolverJam: Solving Problems with Tech and AI on 10th May 2025. It is part of a 6-week program called Digital Tools for Social Change: Problem-Solving with Tech & AI. The goal of this program is to help us learn how to solve real-world problems using technology and artificial intelligence. The session was hosted by Gautamraj Sir and supported by Chiku Sir and Navneet Ma’am.

The meeting began with Chiku Sir explaining the agenda. After that, we moved into breakout rooms where we introduced ourselves by sharing our names, the meanings of our names, and whether we had heard about or used AI before.

Once we came back from the breakout rooms, Gautamraj Sir led an interesting activity where we had to guess if a picture was made by AI or a real one. It was fun, and we learned how to spot the difference between original and AI-generated images.

After this, we had a Learning by Doing session called Tech Mapping. In this activity, we drew a rough outline of our house on a piece of paper. Then, we walked around our house to identify two most used and two least used tech or non-tech items—like fans, mixers, sofas, or digital devices—and marked them on our drawing. We also listed the top 5 mobile apps used in our homes. Later, we shared our tech maps and thoughts with our teammates in breakout rooms. It was fun and helped us understand how much we use different tools in daily life.

After the session, we were asked to download Google Classroom. We also received a link to join the classroom for future assignments and updates.

🎯👉We then got our Week 1 Challenge. The theme is How is technology changing your street? For this, we have to observe, collect data, and talk to people in our area using tools like a phone camera, notebook, and voice recorder. The challenge will take place from May 10 to May 17, 2025.

Before starting the Tech Walk, we also spent time preparing our survey questions to make sure we asked the right things and collected useful information.

🎯👉I successfully completed and submitted my assignment on 14th May 2025, where I collaborated with my peers Vabek and Armaan to conduct a Tech Walk near Doaba College. We interacted with local vendors including a Parshad Shop inside Devi Talab Mandir, Dr. Deo’z Pizza & Cafe, and a tea stall near Kamal Hospital. Through these surveys, we observed different levels of technology use—ranging from CCTV cameras, QR payments, and biometric systems to resistance due to language barriers or banking issues. The insights highlighted how tech adoption varies based on need, awareness, and accessibility.

🎯👉On 15th May 2025, I also participated in the Mentoring Clinic session on Zoom, hosted by Chiku Sir, Gautamraj Sir, and Navneet Ma’am. The session began with an introduction activity in the chat, followed by sharing our biggest question for the mentors. We then visualized two scenarios: a day with everything using technology and a day without any technology, and shared two related images. Participants also created and discussed images generated using AI tools. After that, we were divided into breakout rooms where we reflected on our observations and findings from the Week 1 challenge. Upon returning to the main room, one representative from each breakout room shared the key points discussed. The session ended with a group photo to capture the moment.

To motivate more participants to share their stories, I also created and shared a fun meme in our group encouraging everyone to open up about their first tech experience.

It was an exciting and insightful start to the program, and I’m looking forward to the upcoming weeks of learning and applying technology for social good.

Title: SBM-U 2.0: Community Toilet ODF+ Maintenance Audit
User_Story_x:
👉👉 During Week 4 of the SolverJam program, I Lakshay Behal received the fourth weekly challenge on June 1, 2025, focused on the Solution Stage. This week’s task was to creatively brainstorm and test actionable solutions to the problem we investigated in Week 3 — the overuse of plastic stick files by college students. Using AI tools, we explored a variety of ideas to reduce single-use plastic in academic submissions. My team and I used AI support to generate five creative and practical ideas: setting up an Eco-File Kiosk, drafting a “No Plastic File” policy, hosting DIY Sustainable File Workshops, offering a Starter Eco-Kit for Students, and launching a Green Submissions Week Awareness & Pledge Campaign.

To evaluate these ideas effectively, we created a Mind Map to classify them based on their implementation feasibility. Under the “✅ Doable Now” category, we included classroom awareness campaigns, poster drives, and faculty engagement. The “🤝 Needs Partners” section listed collaborations with eco-vendors and local NGOs or artisans for workshops. The “🔬 Needs More Research” section explored cost comparisons of plastic vs. eco-files, durability studies, and student preferences.

After discussion, we finalized the Awareness & Pledge Campaign as our selected solution. This campaign focuses on engaging students and faculty through creative messaging, posters, social media, and classroom talks. It also encourages students to take a pledge and share their commitment publicly. We designed step-by-step testing strategies that included visuals, pledge walls or QR codes, and feedback collection to evaluate the impact. This idea was chosen for its ease of execution, ability to generate peer influence, and strong potential to create long-term behavior change. It directly addresses the lack of awareness and builds community ownership around the issue of plastic waste in college submissions.

👉👉 On June 5, we attended the fourth mentoring clinic. The meeting was hosted by Chiku Sir along with Gautamraj Sir. It started with an activity titled “If your problem was a movie title, what would it be?” In this task, we were asked to first create a movie title from our problem statement with the help of AI, and then generate a movie poster based on that title. Everyone posted their prompts and posters in the WhatsApp group, after which we discussed them. Gautamraj Sir provided feedback on how we could improve our prompts, and we then revised them and created new posters.

Gautamraj Sir explained the importance of prompts and how significantly they can influence the outcome. We also discussed why there was low participation in some areas and explored what could be improved moving forward. The meeting ended with a group photo. This session helped me understand the value of crafting strong prompts and how they can shape better, more creative solutions.

Title: SBM-U 2.0: Community Toilet ODF+ Maintenance Audit
User_Story_x:
I conceptualized an idea to disseminate knowledge about child rights using WhatsApp bots, leveraging the platform's accessibility and user-friendly interface to reach a broad audience. The goal was to create an interactive and engaging flow that would not only educate participants but also empower them to take actionable steps toward safeguarding child rights.

The Flow:
Learning Module
The journey begins with participants engaging in a brief learning session about child rights. This module provides concise, easy-to-understand information about fundamental rights, examples of violations, and real-world scenarios.

Immersive Testing
Following the learning phase, participants would be placed in hypothetical situations where the rights of children were compromised. These scenarios are designed to be immersive, encouraging participants to empathize by imagining themselves as the affected child.

Participants would then be asked to respond to questions such as:

"If you were this child, what action would you hope someone takes to help you?"
"As a bystander or community member, what would you do to address this issue?"
Their responses would not only test their understanding but also inspire proactive thinking and empathy.

Action Points and Recognition
At the end of the flow, participants would receive a curated list of actionable steps they can take to support and promote child rights in their communities. This might include reporting violations, supporting advocacy groups, or spreading awareness.

To incentivize and recognize their participation, they would be awarded a digital badge titled "Rights Champion", symbolizing their commitment to child rights. This badge could be shared on social media, encouraging others to join the initiative.

Impact:
This idea aims to blend education, empathy-building, and action into one seamless experience. By utilizing WhatsApp bots, we ensure accessibility, as most people are already familiar with the platform. The interactive format ensures that participants actively engage with the content rather than passively consuming information.

Ultimately, this initiative seeks to create a network of informed and motivated individuals who are equipped to identify, address, and prevent child rights violations, fostering a culture of accountability and care.

👉👉 For this, I prepared a PPT presentation capturing our full journey—from identifying the issue, conducting a survey with 45 students, testing three eco-friendly alternatives, to submitting a proposal letter with 33 student signatures to the HOD. 👉👉 We officially submitted our PPT titled “Plastic File Replacement Project” on June 19, 2025, highlighting the problem, data, solutions, feedback, and impact.

Each participant contributed one sentence to a shared, fictional story—mixing imagination, emotion, and fun. I contributed a whimsical tale inspired by my SolverJam experience in Jalandhar.
👉👉Then,we reflected on “Your Journey”—why we chose our problem, what we learned, and how we’ve grown over six weeks. It made me realize how a simple issue like plastic file use led to a larger shift in mindset and confidence. 

👉👉 We wrapped up the mentoring clinic, with a group photo, capturing not just faces—but the spirit of a community that’s learning, experimenting, and creating impact together.
👉👉 Overall, Week 6 was about storytelling, reflection, and realization. It helped me understand how small actions can drive systemic change and how storytelling can amplify impact. The mentoring session and final challenge reminded me that our voice and journey matter—and sharing them can inspire more changemakers.

Title: SBM-U 2.0: Community Toilet ODF+ Maintenance Audit
User_Story_x:
📅 On 22 August 2025, I completed my weekly action challenge given by Reap Benefit. This week’s task focused on creating awareness around #AnimalSafetyForUs, an issue that is often misunderstood in our communities. Every day we hear about dog bites, but instead of asking why they happen, the usual response is to remove or relocate community dogs. Through my LinkedIn post, I wanted to highlight that this is not a real solution — in fact, it creates bigger problems.

📢 In my post, I explained the concept of the “vacuum effect,” where new, unvaccinated dogs enter the area once existing ones are removed, often leading to more aggression and outbreaks of diseases such as parvo, distemper, and leptospirosis. I also used real-world examples: experts in #DelhiNCR have already warned that removing dogs can reverse progress on rabies control, and former Union Minister Maneka Gandhi reminded us of how 19th-century #Paris, after removing dogs and cats, faced a devastating rat infestation. These stories helped me connect facts with urgency.

💚 My campaign stressed that instead of removal, the real solutions are sterilisation, vaccination, and creating feeding spots. These actions not only keep dogs healthy but also make communities safer. I encouraged people to talk about this issue with their #Neighbours, #Families, and #Communities, and to join the campaign by using the hashtag #AnimalSafetyForUs.

🌍 Posting on LinkedIn gave me a platform to share a strong message that combined data, real-life cases, and human emotion. It was not just about animals, but about our own safety, public health, and compassion. With this action, I completed my weekly challenge from Reap Benefit, showing how digital advocacy can shape public opinion and inspire community-driven solutions.

Title: Road Safety & MoRTH Blackspot Mitigation Audit
User_Story_x:
In June 2023, I discussed my work with social worker Varshatai over the phone. In the Yavatmal district, Varshatai works on various topics. But his main work is to make young girls aware of sanitary pads. After that Varshatai wished me good luck with my work as she was a bit shocked to see my WhatsApp Dp and work as I work so much thinking at such a young age. After that, when Varshatai asked me about my work, I explained my work to Tai, then Tai told me that she is personally working in the villages in Yavatmal district like Madkona, Bhari, Kalamb, and Shivani, etc. to create awareness about the use of sanitary pads by girls and women Because in the course of her other work, she noticed that some girls and women in the above villages still use the cloth available at home during menstruation. 

Then Tai told me that she guides women and girls to use sanitary pads. Then she said that a plan was made to provide sanitary pads to women and girls free of cost. Environmental damage is caused due to these sanitary pads. As they know that pads do not decompose for 300-800 years, they have compromised with some shareholders to get the sanitary pads incinerator at a low cost. She said that. So I mixed her work and my work and explained that she was mainly working on women's health and not harming the environment, and I was also working on women's and girls' health as well as not harming the environment. Both have a lot of similarities in their work. But there seems to be a slight difference in the work of both. I am leading women and girls away from using sanitary pads. Varshatai is guiding women and girls to use sanitary pads. Then I told Tai that our work is similar. But I felt a little different in our work. If on the one hand, we are paying attention to avoid environmental damage and on the other hand we are providing free sanitary pads to girls, then this is not right. 

To this, Varshatai said to me that there is no other solution, so I am buying a sanitary pads incinerator which is available at a low price. Also, when Varshatai asked me if there was another solution for this, I told her two options were cloth pads and menstrual cups. Varsha Tai did not understand this. So I said to be more clear that you are creating awareness and also guiding about using sanitary pads among women and girls right in that place. Here you can create awareness among girls about cloth pads and menstrual cups. This can help you achieve your goal of protecting women's health because using sanitary pads has been shown to cause most illnesses. Like rashes, screen infections, and even cancer. This will also protect the environment. If we train women in the future how to make clothes,  women will also get employment. Thus I planned with Varsha Tai and she also agreed.


On June 6, 2023, my classmate Akshay who is currently working on the topic of health in the Gandhi fellowship. I sent them a Google form to fill out the video-related information. He then guided me to make clothes pads from the fiber of the banana testator and he informed me about an industry started by one Anuj Ramatri in Madhya Pradesh. Also sent some videos about making clothes pads.
User_Story_y:
Complexity: High | Time: 4 Weeks | Resources: Road safety guide, accident logs, measuring tape, copy of IRC:99.

THE STORY
I identified a high-risk intersection in my locality that met the Ministry of Road Transport and Highways (MoRTH) criteria for a dangerous ‘Blackspot.’ I led a youth team to audit the lack of signage and traffic-calming measures to propose life-saving engineering changes.

THE GOAL
To achieve official recognition of the blackspot and secure installation of IRC-compliant traffic calming (rumble strips/signage).

THE STEPS
1. Hazard Mapping: Identify a 300m road stretch with high accident history. Log the exact GPS coordinates of the intersection.
2. Infrastructure Audit: Use IRC:99 standards to check for rumble strips, ‘Danger Ahead’ signs, and proper road markings.
3. Visibility Audit: Map all ‘Fixed Obstructions’ (Poles, Trees, Billboards) that block the line-of-sight for oncoming vehicles.
4. Formal Proposal: Draft a ‘Blackspot Mitigation Proposal’ and submit it to the Traffic Police DCP and the PWD Executive Engineer.

WHAT TO SAY
"Our data shows this 300m stretch has seen [Number] accidents in 3 years, qualifying as a MoRTH Blackspot. Per IRC:99, we request immediate installation of raised thermoplastic rumble strips and improved signage."

OVERCOMING PROBLEMS
* The Problem: Authorities might say they don’t have the budget for a full new signal.
* The Fix: Propose low-cost ‘Tactical Urbanism’ fixes: rumble strips, solar blinkers, and high-visibility zebra markings which can be done quickly.

SUCCESS
The Traffic Police officially added the location to their blackspot monitoring list and installed solar blinkers within 15 days.
Gap_Analysis_y:
Specific Gap: The audit lacks a technical check of signage reflectivity (Night Visibility). Fix: Add a night-time visibility test for road signs.
Specific Gap: No direct linkage between speeding data and lack of rumble strips. Fix: Log vehicle speeds at peak hours vs standard limits.
Specific Gap: Lacks a formal check of pedestrian refuge islands at wide intersections. Fix: Provide a ‘Pedestrian Safety’ audit template.
Specific Gap: No tracking of municipal resolve time once reported. Fix: Provide a Civic Resolve Tracker.
Admin_Fix_FullText: [TEMPLATE: BLACKSPOT AUDIT] Columns: Stretch ID | Hazards Identified | Signage Check | Speeding Data. [LEGAL FIX: IRC:99] Provides guidelines for traffic calming measures in urban areas.
Admin_Fix_Source: MoRTH Blackspot Protocol / IRC:99 Traffic Calming Guidelines

Title: Road Safety & MoRTH Blackspot Mitigation Audit
User_Story_x:
Meeting with Ward officer- J.O. of my community
-Visited N ward and got to know MCGM has started recruiting the NGO for sanitation before the monsoon
- In the budget of MCGM.Under the Slum sanitation program. It is planned to construct 20,000 new toilets under Lot-12 in 2023-24. Also, approximately 3000 toilets constructed in the slums by the MHADA authority will be operated and maintained by the Corporation. 
- then contacted the solid waste management department and got an appointment with the J.O of my area.
Meeting with Ward officer- J.O. of my community
During the discussion I conveyed to the officer I wanted to know about sanitation programs in my area that MCGM is planning to get to know they are planning to have a toilet per home and will be in action sooner, then I focus on the people’s participation. They were ready for the session in my community.
The officer was surprised as no residents came and asked for help. Then he willingly helped me and took my number and said whenever the session will be conducted he will be there.
Meeting with Sanitation contractor 
The sanitation contractor and I had a discussion about the sanitation status, then he started asking me to take other communities as politically it is very dangerous and it was a very threatening type of conversation. But I calmly replied and in the end, he shared that we are able to plan new initiatives in our community along with the challenges the sanitation workers faced and people's participation in the process.
Later he replied to me that after arranging the session he will come and give a few ideas to put a dustbin in the toilets, make demand for mobile toilets for physically challenged people.
Visited NGO- PUKAR, PRAJA, BRM and Panni Haq samiti 
Visited the organisation as they also work on sanitation issues. I received the data of N ward of sanitation complaints from the past 3 years. It is s2760, 1574, and 1666 respectively. Also able to read and understand the possible ways like awarding the people about their rights. Also help me to handle the political situation and keep away from affecting the process by being transparent.

In May month, I got a chance to present my problem statement with the Minister of Women and Child  Development and Mangalprabhat Lodha as they visited Mumbai Municipal Corporation 'N ward of MCGM’. After that I had a chance to discuss with the Assistant head and Assistant engineer of Solid waste department where sir shared the importance of sanitation awareness among communities.

My name is Sukhmanpreet Kaur, and I am a proud member of the Change Maker League in my school. On 31st May, our school organized a Parent-Teacher Meeting (PTM). As responsible members of the Change Maker League, we took an initiative to make this event plastic-free.

To serve water to the parents and guests, we used steel glasses instead of single-use plastic or paper cups. This small step helped us reduce plastic waste and promote sustainable practices within our school community.

I was wearing my Solve Ninja shirt, and my teammates wore their Solve Ninja Bot shirts. Many people noticed our badges and shirts, and they got curious. When they asked us about Solve Ninja, we happily explained everything — how we are part of a movement that encourages young students to identify problems in their community and solve them using simple, eco-friendly, and innovative solutions.

After sharing this information, we felt very proud. This was the third plastic-free event we successfully organized in our school. It made us realize that small steps, when taken together, can lead to big changes. We are committed to continuing our efforts to make our school and community more sustainable and environmentally friendly.

Title: Road Safety & MoRTH Blackspot Mitigation Audit
User_Story_x:
Creating awareness about menstrual health and hygiene is essential in improving the well-being of women and adolescent girls. Menstrual health is intricately linked to the environment and sustainability.
In Feb 2023, one of the volunteers conducted a survey and an investigation of the harmful health effects of certain menstrual products. It was concluded that most of these products caused rashes and other backterial infections in adolescent girls. According to the report 'Menstrual Waste 2022' (India), sanitary napkins contain chemicals called Phthalates, which are also used to make plastics that can cause cancer, infertility, PCOD and endometriosis. According to a report by an environmental group called 'Toxic Links', 1230 crore sanitary pads were disposed off in 2021. A sanitary pad contains plastic equivalent to 4 plastic bags, which takes about 250 to 800 years to degrade.

For more information on menstrual health and hygiene click on the following links:
https://www.unicef.org/wash/menstrual-hygiene
https://tinyurl.com/yc3apny8

Title: Residential Energy Efficiency & BEE Appliance Audit
User_Story_x:
📅 On 8th August 2025, I officially registered for Solver Jam – Jalandhar, scheduled for 24th August at Edumust Office, Model Town, Jalandhar. As a Punjab Core Team Member, I took responsibility for outreach and participant confirmations for this event. This marked the beginning of my active involvement in ensuring the success of the session. 📝

📢 On 18th August, I received the official poster and invitation message from the team. I immediately started outreach by sharing it with 22 individuals on WhatsApp (mostly my college peers and civic campaign friends), in 3 WhatsApp groups (College Friends, Garbage Spot Fix Campaign, BCA Info), and 3 Instagram groups (including Civic Environmental Action Squad). Later, on 19th August, I extended this outreach by posting in 2 more groups — Civic Environmental Action Squad and Jalandhar Solve Squad. Through this wide circulation, I ensured maximum visibility for the upcoming Solver Jam. 🌍

💬 On 21st August, I joined the Solver Jam Sync-up Meeting hosted by Bharat Sir, along with Chiku Sir, Vabek, and Prince. We discussed the detailed planning for Solver Jam Jalandhar. My key tasks were clarified: conducting outreach in my college and taking confirmations from registered participants. I also nudged Bharat Sir for the participant list to begin follow-ups. The same day, I shared with him the detailed report of my outreach — student list, WhatsApp groups, and Instagram groups where I had spread the message. This gave transparency and clarity to my efforts. ✅

📞 On 22nd August, I began direct confirmation follow-ups. I crafted a polite message asking the 21 students I had outreached earlier if they would attend. Responses started coming in: Vishali confirmed, while Kanchan, Hardik, Arpana, and Pardeep declined. Simran (KMV) said she would “try to join,” while others were pending. This step helped filter out the actual participants who could attend.

📋 On 23rd August, Bharat Sir shared with me the list of registered participants, and I began calling them. Parveen Kaur confirmed, while Shubham Singh said he’d call back (later he declined), Nisha didn’t pick up, and Sandeep Singh cut the call. I also created and shared a poll message in the group to track who would come the next day. Later that day, I shared an updated confirmation vs. cancellation list with Bharat Sir and, on his guidance, circulated the invite in the Eco Club group for more outreach. 📲

🌞 On the morning of 24th August, I shared a fun poll message in the Jalandhar Solve Squad group: “Nikal gaye ghar se 🏃 / Nikalne wale hain 🧳 / Abhi to aaram se 😌 / Ayenge 🚶 / Ayenge hi nahi 🙈”. This lighthearted check-in helped build excitement and gave us a real-time idea of participant status. I also followed up with Anjali (sending her the location) and called Khwahish, who said she was unsure about attending.

🎉 Finally, the Solver Jam – Jalandhar event was facilitated by Bharat Sir. It began with a feeling check-in and an ice-breaking game where we jumped on “potholes,” clapped on “horn,” and froze on “traffic.” This created a fun, high-energy atmosphere. Bharat Sir then explained the theme — tackling potholes — and shared his personal story of reporting a pothole using the mSeva WhatsApp chatbot in Jagraon. The idea was to experience civic problem-solving hands-on by reporting potholes, but due to chatbot lag, we couldn’t complete it live.  Back at the Edumust office, we reflected, and Sir explained how to record action on CMP. Still, the process and storytelling made the session enjoyable and insightful. 🌟

🙌 Overall, my journey for Solver Jam Jalandhar was a mix of outreach, confirmations, follow-ups, and group coordination, ending in an experiential session where participants learned problem-solving in a fun, civic-action way. This experience sharpened my skills in communication, outreach strategy, and event coordination, while also giving me the joy of contributing to a real change-making process.
User_Story_y:
Complexity: Low | Time: 1 Week | Resources: Electricity bill (past 6 months), BEE Star Label guide, smart-meter (optional).

THE STORY
I analyzed our household electricity consumption to identify ‘Energy Leaks’ and poor-performing appliances. By auditing our star-rated appliances and comparing energy cycles, I successfully proposed a low-cost retrofit plan that reduced our monthly bill by 15%.

THE GOAL
To achieve a 15% reduction in household energy consumption through appliance efficiency and load management.

THE STEPS
1. Bill Analysis: Map your energy consumption (kWh) for the last 6 months. Identify the peak usage months (e.g., Summer/AC usage).
2. Appliance Audit: List all major appliances (AC, Refrigerator, Geyser). Check their BEE Star Rating. Older non-star appliances are the ‘leaks’.
3. Phantom Load Test: Turn off all switches but leave appliances plugged in. Map the ‘Phantom Load’ (vampire power) and use master-switches to eliminate it.
4. Behavioral Fix: Implement a ‘Off-Peak’ usage schedule for high-wattage appliances (e.g., running the geyser/pump during non-peak utility hours).

WHAT TO SAY
"Our audit shows that we have two 2-star appliances and significant phantom loads. By switching to 5-star rated units and using master switches, we can save [Amount] annually while reducing our carbon footprint."

OVERCOMING PROBLEMS
* The Problem: Initial cost of upgrading to 5-star appliances is high.
* The Fix: Calculate the ‘Payback Period.’ Show that the energy savings will pay for the new appliance in 18-24 months.

SUCCESS
Achieved a verifiable 15% drop in monthly electricity usage as per the official DISCOM bill.
Gap_Analysis_y:
Specific Gap: The action lacks a technical calculation for ‘Energy Payback Period’ (Cost vs Savings). Fix: Provide a simple ROI Calculator template.
Specific Gap: No direct linkage to the State Electricity Regulatory Commission (SERC) consumer rights. Fix: Add a ‘Consumer Bill Dispute’ SOP.
Specific Gap: Lacks a formal check for renewable energy potential (Solar PV). Fix: Provide a ‘Solar Feasibility’ 3-step audit.
Specific Gap: No tracking of long-term behavioral compliance. Fix: Provide a Monthly Energy Habit Tracker.
Admin_Fix_FullText: [TEMPLATE: ENERGY AUDIT] Columns: Appliance | Age | BEE Stars | Daily Hours | Est. kWh. [LEGAL FIX: BEE STANDARDS] Reference the Eco Niwas Samhita guidelines for residential efficiency.
Admin_Fix_Source: Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) / State DISCOM Charter

Title: Residential Energy Efficiency & BEE Appliance Audit
User_Story_x:
📅 On 13th July 2025, as part of the weekly action given by Reap Benefit, I took action in the Campaigner role under the Safe Roads = Safe Students campaign.  I was deeply concerned after observing unsafe road conditions near schools and colleges in Jalandhar—faded zebra crossings, lack of speed breakers, and careless driving near educational institutions. These everyday risks motivated me to take this issue forward as part of my weekly campaigner action.

📸 I started by identifying specific road zones around Jalandhar where student safety was clearly compromised. I then created a poster highlighting the danger posed by a nearly invisible zebra crossing used by hundreds of students daily. This poster was posted on LinkedIn to amplify the issue and bring it to the attention of both the public and the authorities.

📬 In my LinkedIn post, I tagged @MCJalandhar and shared an example of how small actions like repainting a zebra crossing can save lives. I also included a recent reference from UNICEF & NIMHANS’ 2025 report, which reveals that 45 children die every day in India due to road crashes. This data added urgency to the message and grounded it in facts.

📣 I encouraged others to take similar action—observe their surroundings, share visuals, tag civic leaders, and start conversations. The goal was to inspire more people to speak up about traffic safety around schools and to make this a collective movement rather than an individual effort.

Title: Residential Energy Efficiency & BEE Appliance Audit
User_Story_x:

User_Story_y:
Complexity: Low | Time: 1 Week | Resources: Electricity bill (past 6 months), BEE Star Label guide, smart-meter (optional).

THE STORY
I analyzed our household electricity consumption to identify ‘Energy Leaks’ and poor-performing appliances. By auditing our star-rated appliances and comparing energy cycles, I successfully proposed a low-cost retrofit plan that reduced our monthly bill by 15%.

THE GOAL
To achieve a 15% reduction in household energy consumption through appliance efficiency and load management.

THE STEPS
1. Bill Analysis: Map your energy consumption (kWh) for the last 6 months. Identify the peak usage months (e.g., Summer/AC usage).
2. Appliance Audit: List all major appliances (AC, Refrigerator, Geyser). Check their BEE Star Rating. Older non-star appliances are the ‘leaks’.
3. Phantom Load Test: Turn off all switches but leave appliances plugged in. Map the ‘Phantom Load’ (vampire power) and use master-switches to eliminate it.
4. Behavioral Fix: Implement a ‘Off-Peak’ usage schedule for high-wattage appliances (e.g., running the geyser/pump during non-peak utility hours).

WHAT TO SAY
"Our audit shows that we have two 2-star appliances and significant phantom loads. By switching to 5-star rated units and using master switches, we can save [Amount] annually while reducing our carbon footprint."

OVERCOMING PROBLEMS
* The Problem: Initial cost of upgrading to 5-star appliances is high.
* The Fix: Calculate the ‘Payback Period.’ Show that the energy savings will pay for the new appliance in 18-24 months.

SUCCESS
Achieved a verifiable 15% drop in monthly electricity usage as per the official DISCOM bill.

Specific Gap: The action lacks a technical calculation for ‘Energy Payback Period’ (Cost vs Savings). Fix: Provide a simple ROI Calculator template.

Title: RTI-Driven Accountability for Local Civic Works
User_Story_x:
I, Lakshay Behal, was part of the core team that organized Solver Jam Jalandhar, held on 22nd June 2025. The planning began on 9th June, and I contributed actively from day one.

👉👉
In the initial phase, our team finalized the event date and shortlisted venues—Devi Talab Mandir and the Edumust office (offered by Edumust Sir during our LPU NSS Fair visit). On 11th June, I prepared and shared a message listing key planning aspects: venue, timing, sessions, snacks, materials, and documentation.

👉👉
On 13th June, we had a site visit with Bharat Sir and Prince at the Edumust office. The day turned out to be extremely productive—we conducted three street audits, discussed session flow, and planned logistics. We also enjoyed lunch with the Edumust team, which strengthened our collaboration. This meetup set the tone for the event’s quality and purpose.

👉👉
I supported registration testing and gave feedback on both the initial and final versions of the registration form. I also led promotions, sharing the poster and invite in multiple groups—Jalandhar and Ludhiana Solve Squads, college groups, the 30-Day Environmental Challenge group, and sent individual messages to 20+ people. I reached out to both NSS and Eco Club faculty, ensuring official circulation after follow-ups.

👉👉
Ahead of the event, I initiated a poll to finalize timing for the dry run, created the Google Meet link, and shared it with the team. On 20th June, we held the dry run where Bharat Sir walked us through the timeline, team roles, and strategies to engage participants. I was also tasked with utilizing Yoga Day at my college to promote registrations. I spoke to students, got 2 registered, and distributed posters with help from my classmate Vabek. I also printed event materials (such as posters) and arranged refreshments (bananas and Tang).

👉👉
On 22nd June, I managed on-ground responsibilities from the beginning. I sent out a fun status check-in poll and, along with other core members, made confirmation calls to all registrants to ensure smooth attendance. We welcomed participants, and joined the session flow led by Bharat Sir.

The event kicked off with an ice-breaker game, followed by participant introductions and reflections on “Why are you here?” and “What issues do you observe in your streets?” We then formed three teams for a street audit, where we observed the streets, captured photos of key issues, and shared them in the Jalandhar Solve Squad group. As part of the audit, each team was required to have conversations with at least two people—our team interacted with vendors, citizens, and guards to better understand on-ground civic challenges.

👉👉
After returning from the audit, we had refreshments (bananas and Tang), followed by filling out the 17-question street audit form through the Solve Ninja WhatsApp bot. We waited until all participants had submitted their responses.

👉👉
Post-audit, we held a reflection session where each participant wrote their learning on chart paper. These were randomly picked and shared, creating a dynamic and open discussion space. The energy and engagement were deeply positive and memorable.

👉👉
Finally, we introduced the Jalandhar Solve Squad, the CMP (ChangeMaker Profile) platform, and the opportunities available to them. We explained how they could use the platform to track their actions and personal growth.

👉👉
After the event concluded and participants left, our core team had lunch together. 

👉👉
Later, our core team gathered and had a feedback and reflection meet at a nearby park to reflect on what went well, what could be improved, and to identify key moments where participant experience was clearly positive—such as active engagement, meaningful conversations, and enthusiastic sharing.

👉👉
To wrap up the event, I created a WhatsApp group for all participants to complete their pre-assessment and stay connected for future activities.

👉👉
Thank you Reap Benefit and Bharat Sir for your continuous support, mentorship, and for trusting me with this opportunity.
User_Story_y:

THE STORY

Title: RTI-Driven Accountability for Local Civic Works
User_Story_x:
Recently I participated in offline Solverjam in Jalandhar. I was part of the core team that organized the SolverJam Jalandhar held on 22nd June 2025. The planning began on 9th June, and I actively contributed from the very beginning. Our first tasks included finalizing the event date and shortlisting venues. 
In the following days, I contributed to registration testing, gave feedback, and helped launch the final poster and link. I took ownership of promotions, sharing the poster in various groups: Jalandhar and Ludhiana Solve Squads, college groupa etc.
On 20 June bharat sir organized dry run, During the call, Bharat Sir explained roles and participant engagement strategies. I was assigned a task to promote registrations during our college’s Yoga Day. 
As core team members, we also took the responsibility to call all registered participants to confirm whether they were attending or not. This helped us get a clear idea of turnout and manage preparations accordingly. Once participants started arriving, we welcomed them and ensured a smooth check-in. The day began with an ice-breaking game by Bharat Sir, followed by intros and the question “Why are you here?”. Participants then shared civic issues they observe on streets. We were divided into three teams to conduct a street audit, observed local problems, and interacted with vendors, citizens, and guards. Post-audit, we had a reflection activity where everyone wrote learnings on chart paper and presented them. Finally, sir introduced the participants to the Solve Squad, CMP platform, and how they can track their actions and growth. After the event, our core team had a feedback session in a nearby park, reflecting on what went well, where we could improve, and what resonated with participants. It was such a jaw dropping experience and I had a great time.

Title: RTI-Driven Accountability for Local Civic Works
User_Story_x:
A street audit or investigation was conducted by Solve Ninja, where they Created Lightmeter to measure light intensity of streetlights. Team Galli Savings and Photon discovered that the streetlights of their layout were not functioning well and this was hindering their evening activities. Upon research they found out that poor lighting causes 15% of road accidents.
With these findings, the team devised a solution called Lightmeter which will measure the light intensity of the streetlights with the help of another Solve Ninja named Rajshekhar who has been working on solving a similar issue.
They understood and learned how to build the lightmeter and also going to work on mapping the poor light intensity areas in the forthcoming week and will be presenting the data collected to the MLA of the area with the hope to get it fixed!

Title: RTI-Driven Accountability for Local Civic Works
User_Story_x: I conducted three street audits in Anjanpura town, specifically in the Badavane area, and observed that the streetlights have not been functioning for the past six months. During my investigation, I spoke to more than 30 individuals to gain deeper insights. We discovered that three streetlights were not working. It became apparent that over 300 people are experiencing difficulties during nighttime due to the non-functional streetlights, but many are unaware of the proper reporting procedures. After reporting the issue to the Bescom authority, they promptly fixed the three non-functional streetlights.

User_Story_y:

THE STORY
I docuemnted hazardous electrical wiring and open transformers in my locality to ensure a safe environment for all residents.

THE GOAL
To neutralize 3 critical electrical fire/safety hazards in the ward.

THE STEPS
1. Mapping: Tag open transformers with unique Asset IDs.
2. Safety Audit: Check vertical clearance of h-tension wires.
3. Reporting: Call 1912 and photo-log the complaint.

Specific Gap: Lacks a tracking mechanism for municipal follow-ups. Fix: Provide a Civic Tracking Log template.
